nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.label: AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.excerpt: AI agents use models to choose actions, call tools, observe results, and continue toward a goal. Tool use turns an LLM from a text generator into a system that can retrieve data, execute operations, and affect external state.

nodes.label: AI Alignment Overview
nodes.excerpt: AI alignment is the problem of making AI systems pursue goals and behaviors that match human intent, values, and safety constraints. It includes instruction following, truthfulness, robustness, oversight, and control.

nodes.label: AI Memory Architectures
nodes.excerpt: AI memory architectures organize how agents store, retrieve, update, and use information over time. Useful memory systems combine short-term context, long-term retrieval, summaries, user preferences, and safety controls.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.label: AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.excerpt: AI interpretability research tries to understand what neural networks represent internally and why they produce particular outputs. It supports safety by making model behavior less opaque and easier to audit.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.label: Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.excerpt: Activation functions introduce nonlinear behavior into neural networks and shape how signals flow through layers. ReLU, GELU, sigmoid, tanh, and softmax each serve different roles in modern models.

nodes.label: Attention Mechanism — Self-Attention
nodes.excerpt: Self-attention lets each token in a sequence dynamically read information from other tokens. It is the core mechanism that allows transformers to build contextual representations.

nodes.label: Backpropagation Explained
nodes.excerpt: Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes gradients for neural network parameters efficiently. It applies the chain rule backward through the network so optimizers know how to reduce loss.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.label: Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.excerpt: Batch normalization and layer normalization stabilize neural network training by normalizing intermediate activations. BatchNorm is common in CNNs, while LayerNorm is central to transformers and sequence models.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.label: Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.excerpt: AI benchmarks are standardized tasks used to compare model capabilities. MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, and GPQA test different slices of knowledge, coding, math reasoning, and expert-level question answering.

nodes.label: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
nodes.excerpt: Chain-of-thought prompting encourages a model to work through intermediate reasoning steps before answering. It can improve complex task performance, but production systems often need concise hidden reasoning or structured scratchpads rather than verbose explanations.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.label: Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.excerpt: Chunking strategies decide how documents are split before retrieval in RAG systems. Good chunks preserve enough context to answer questions while staying small enough for precise search and prompt budgets.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.label: Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.excerpt: The Claude API exposes Anthropic's Claude models through a Messages-style interface and official SDKs. Practical use centers on model selection, message structure, max tokens, streaming, tool use, safety, and cost control.

nodes.label: Constitutional AI
nodes.excerpt: Constitutional AI trains models using explicit principles that guide critique, revision, and preference selection. It aims to improve helpfulness and harmlessness while reducing dependence on direct human labeling for every case.

nodes.label: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
nodes.excerpt: Convolutional neural networks use learned filters to detect local spatial patterns in images and grid-like data. Their inductive bias makes them efficient for vision tasks such as classification, detection, and segmentation.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.label: Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Cosine similarity and distance metrics define how vector representations are compared. The metric choice affects semantic search, clustering, recommendations, and nearest-neighbor retrieval quality.

nodes.label: Diffusion Models
nodes.excerpt: Diffusion models generate data by learning to reverse a gradual noising process. They are widely used for image, audio, and video generation because they produce high-quality samples with controllable conditioning.

nodes.label: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
nodes.excerpt: Direct Preference Optimization trains language models directly from preference pairs without fitting a separate reward model and running an RL loop. It is a simpler alternative to classic RLHF for many alignment workflows.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.label: Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.excerpt: Emergent abilities are capabilities that appear suddenly or become measurable only after models reach sufficient scale. The concept is debated because some emergence reflects real phase changes, while some reflects metric and evaluation artifacts.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.label: Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.excerpt: Few-shot and zero-shot learning describe a model's ability to perform tasks with little or no task-specific training data. In LLMs, these capabilities often come from prompting rather than updating model weights.

nodes.label: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
nodes.excerpt: Generative Adversarial Networks train a generator and discriminator in competition. GANs can create sharp samples but are difficult to train and have largely been displaced by diffusion models for many image-generation tasks.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.label: Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.excerpt: Gradient descent variants are optimization algorithms that update model parameters using gradients. SGD, AdaGrad, and Adam differ in how they use noise, learning history, momentum, and per-parameter step sizes.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.label: Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.excerpt: Hallucination is when a model produces unsupported, false, or fabricated output. It comes from next-token generation, incomplete context, weak uncertainty calibration, and objective mismatch, so mitigation requires retrieval, constraints, verification, and good UX.

nodes.label: Knowledge Distillation
nodes.excerpt: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to imitate a larger or stronger teacher model. It can reduce inference cost while preserving much of the teacher's behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.label: Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.excerpt: Local LLMs run open-weight language models on personal or self-managed hardware using tools such as Ollama, llama.cpp, and LM Studio. They trade frontier-model quality for privacy, offline use, customization, and cost control.

nodes.label: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
nodes.excerpt: Long Short-Term Memory networks are recurrent models with gates that control what information is stored, forgotten, and emitted. They were a major solution to long-range dependency problems before transformers.

nodes.label: Loss Functions
nodes.excerpt: Loss functions define what a model is penalized for during training. They convert prediction errors into a scalar objective that optimization algorithms can minimize.

nodes.label: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
nodes.excerpt: Mixture of Experts models route each input token or example through a subset of specialized expert networks. MoE increases total model capacity while keeping per-token compute lower than a dense model of the same size.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.excerpt: Model Context Protocol is an open protocol for connecting AI applications to external tools and context providers. MCP servers expose capabilities such as tools, resources, and prompts through a standardized interface.

nodes.label: Model Evaluation Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Model evaluation metrics measure whether a model is performing the task that matters. Good evaluation separates training loss from real-world quality, reliability, safety, latency, and cost.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.label: Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.excerpt: Model quantization compresses neural network weights and activations into lower-precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. It reduces memory and inference cost, often with some quality tradeoff.

nodes.label: Multi-Head Attention
nodes.excerpt: Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel so a model can capture different relationships at once. Each head learns its own projections and contributes to the layer output.

nodes.label: Multimodal Models
nodes.excerpt: Multimodal models process and relate more than one data type, such as text, images, audio, video, or actions. They make AI systems more useful because real-world tasks rarely arrive as text alone.

nodes.label: Neural Network Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Neural network fundamentals cover how layered mathematical models learn patterns by adjusting weights from data. The core ideas are neurons, parameters, activations, loss, backpropagation, and gradient-based optimization.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.label: OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.excerpt: The OpenAI API provides access to GPT and specialized models through interfaces such as the Responses API. Practical integration involves model choice, structured outputs, tools, streaming, embeddings, safety identifiers, and cost control.

nodes.label: Positional Encoding
nodes.excerpt: Positional encoding gives transformers information about token order. Because self-attention alone is permutation-insensitive, models need positional signals such as sinusoidal embeddings, learned positions, or RoPE.

nodes.label: Pre-training vs Fine-tuning
nodes.excerpt: Pre-training teaches a model broad statistical representations from large datasets, while fine-tuning adapts that model to narrower tasks or behaviors. The distinction explains why modern ML often starts from foundation models instead of training from scratch.

nodes.label: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Prompt engineering is the practice of structuring instructions, context, examples, and constraints so an LLM produces useful outputs. It is less about magic phrasing and more about clear task specification and feedback loops.

nodes.label: RLHF vs RLAIF
nodes.excerpt: RLHF uses human preference feedback while RLAIF uses AI-generated feedback to train model behavior. Both optimize preference signals, but they differ in cost, scalability, bias, oversight, and failure modes.

nodes.label: ReAct Prompting Pattern
nodes.excerpt: ReAct is a prompting pattern that interleaves reasoning and acting. It helps language-model agents decide what to do, call tools, observe results, and continue with updated context.

nodes.label: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
nodes.excerpt: Recurrent neural networks process sequences by carrying hidden state from one step to the next. They were historically important for language, speech, and time series before transformers became dominant.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.label: Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.excerpt: Regularization techniques reduce overfitting by constraining how a model learns from training data. L1, L2, dropout, data augmentation, and early stopping encourage better generalization.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Reinforcement learning trains agents to choose actions that maximize reward over time. Its core frame is agent, environment, state, action, reward, policy, value, and exploration.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.excerpt: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback trains models toward human-preferred behavior using preference data, reward models, and policy optimization. It is a key method behind instruction-following assistants.

nodes.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
nodes.excerpt: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines search with LLM generation by adding relevant external documents to the prompt. It helps models answer with current or private knowledge without retraining.

nodes.label: Scaling Laws
nodes.excerpt: Scaling laws describe predictable relationships between model performance, parameter count, dataset size, and compute. They help labs allocate training budgets and forecast capability improvements.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.label: Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.excerpt: Semantic search retrieves by meaning using embeddings, while keyword search retrieves by exact or lexical term matching. Strong search systems often combine both because each fails in different ways.

nodes.label: Speech Recognition (Whisper, Wav2Vec)
nodes.excerpt: Speech recognition converts audio into text using acoustic modeling, sequence modeling, and language context. Whisper and Wav2Vec represent modern neural approaches that learn robust audio representations.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.label: Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.excerpt: Text-to-speech models convert text into natural-sounding audio. Modern TTS systems model linguistic content, speaker identity, prosody, timing, and waveform generation.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.label: Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.excerpt: Tokenization converts raw text into model-readable token IDs. BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece use subword units so models can handle large vocabularies, rare words, and multilingual text.

nodes.label: Transfer Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer learning reuses knowledge learned on one dataset or task to improve performance on another. It is the reason pre-trained models can be adapted efficiently to new domains with less data.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.label: Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Transformer architecture uses self-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, normalization, and positional information to model sequences in parallel. It is the foundation of modern large language models.

nodes.label: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
nodes.excerpt: Variational Autoencoders learn a structured latent space by encoding data into probability distributions and decoding samples back into data. They are useful for representation learning, generation, and latent compression.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.label: Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.excerpt: Vector databases store embeddings and support fast nearest-neighbor search with metadata filtering. They are commonly used for semantic search, recommendations, and RAG systems.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.label: Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.excerpt: Vector embeddings represent items such as text, images, users, or products as numerical vectors where distance captures semantic similarity. They make semantic search, clustering, recommendations, and RAG retrieval possible.

nodes.label: Vision-Language Models
nodes.excerpt: Vision-language models connect visual inputs with language understanding and generation. They support image captioning, visual question answering, document understanding, UI analysis, and multimodal agents.

nodes.label: AI Workflow
nodes.excerpt: multi-model AI workflow: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, IDE Copilot routing guide.

nodes.label: API Design
nodes.excerpt: REST/RESTful, HTTP semantics, idempotency keys, pagination (cursor vs offset), versioning, auth (JWT/OAuth2), webhooks, REST vs GraphQL/gRPC/WebSockets/SSE/SOAP.

nodes.label: Agent Evaluation
nodes.excerpt: capability benchmarks (SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, OSWorld), safety eval (HarmBench, jailbreak taxonomy, dangerous capability evals), deployment monitoring, A/B testing, evaluation flywheel.

nodes.label: Agent Memory
nodes.excerpt: four memory types (working/episodic/semantic/procedural), Generative Agents retrieval formula, MemGPT, multi-agent memory consistency.

nodes.label: Agent Security
nodes.excerpt: security model for tool-using AI agents: prompt injection, capability-based tools, least privilege, sandboxing, memory poisoning, policy engines, audit logs, and Bobby permission modes.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Aging Biology — Hallmarks of Aging
nodes.label: Aging Biology — Hallmarks of Aging
nodes.excerpt: The hallmarks of aging are biological processes associated with age-related decline, including genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic changes, proteostasis loss, mitochondrial dysfunction, senescence, and altered signaling. The framework is useful but not a menu of proven human interventions.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/CRISPR — How Gene Editing Works
nodes.label: CRISPR — How Gene Editing Works
nodes.excerpt: CRISPR gene editing uses guide RNA and Cas enzymes to target specific DNA sequences for cutting or modification. It is powerful for research and therapy, but delivery, off-target effects, ethics, and long-term monitoring matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Cell Biology Basics (Organelles and Their Roles)
nodes.label: Cell Biology Basics (Organelles and Their Roles)
nodes.excerpt: Cell biology studies the structures and processes that make cells function. Organelles like the nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and membranes divide cellular labor.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Evolution — Natural Selection and Genetic Drift
nodes.label: Evolution — Natural Selection and Genetic Drift
nodes.excerpt: Evolution is change in heritable traits across generations, driven by mechanisms including natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, gene flow, and sexual selection. Selection is not intentional; it filters variation through differential survival and reproduction.

nodes.label: Evolutionary Psychology Overview
nodes.excerpt: Evolutionary psychology studies how evolved pressures may have shaped cognition, emotion, and behavior. It can generate useful hypotheses, but it is vulnerable to just-so stories when evidence is weak.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Genetics — DNA to Protein to Phenotype
nodes.label: Genetics — DNA to Protein to Phenotype
nodes.excerpt: Genetics links DNA sequence, gene expression, proteins, traits, and environment. Phenotype is not a simple one-gene output; it emerges from gene networks, regulation, development, and context.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Hormones Overview (Cortisol, Testosterone, Estrogen, Insulin, GH)
nodes.label: Hormones Overview (Cortisol, Testosterone, Estrogen, Insulin, GH)
nodes.excerpt: Hormones are signaling molecules that coordinate metabolism, stress, reproduction, growth, sleep, and energy balance. Cortisol, testosterone, estrogen, insulin, and growth hormone are context-dependent regulators, not simple good-or-bad chemicals.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Immune System Overview (Innate vs Adaptive)
nodes.label: Immune System Overview (Innate vs Adaptive)
nodes.excerpt: The immune system protects the body through innate fast defenses and adaptive targeted responses. Innate immunity recognizes broad patterns, while adaptive immunity uses B cells, T cells, antibodies, and memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Inflammation — Acute vs Chronic
nodes.label: Inflammation — Acute vs Chronic
nodes.excerpt: Acute inflammation is a short-term protective response to injury or infection, while chronic inflammation persists and can contribute to disease. The goal is regulation and resolution, not eliminating inflammation entirely.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Insulin and Blood Sugar Regulation
nodes.label: Insulin and Blood Sugar Regulation
nodes.excerpt: Insulin helps regulate blood glucose by signaling cells to take up and store nutrients. Blood sugar regulation depends on diet, activity, muscle, liver function, sleep, stress, hormones, and insulin sensitivity.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Longevity Research Overview (Sirtuins, mTOR, Senolytics)
nodes.label: Longevity Research Overview (Sirtuins, mTOR, Senolytics)
nodes.excerpt: Longevity research studies pathways and interventions that might affect healthspan or lifespan, including sirtuins, mTOR, senolytics, caloric restriction, exercise, and metabolic signals. Human evidence is much weaker than animal and mechanistic hype.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/The Stress Response (Fight-or-Flight, Freeze, Fawn)
nodes.label: The Stress Response (Fight-or-Flight, Freeze, Fawn)
nodes.excerpt: The stress response coordinates nervous-system, hormonal, and behavioral patterns such as fight, flight, freeze, and fawn. These responses can be adaptive in threat but costly when chronically activated.

nodes.label: Bobby Commands
nodes.excerpt: cheat sheet of all working Bobby voice commands and known gaps.

nodes.label: Bobby Memory Architecture
nodes.excerpt: ChromaDB + SQLite + Obsidian vault three-layer memory system.

nodes.label: CI CD
nodes.excerpt: CI/CD pipeline stages, GitHub Actions (OIDC, caching, matrix builds), deployment strategies (blue-green, canary, rolling, feature flags), Terraform, ArgoCD/GitOps.

nodes.label: Amortized Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence, without relying on randomness. It explains why occasional expensive operations can still produce efficient long-run behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Arrays and Dynamic Arrays
nodes.label: Arrays and Dynamic Arrays
nodes.excerpt: Arrays store elements contiguously for fast index access, while dynamic arrays resize as they grow. They are simple, cache-friendly, and foundational for many data structures and algorithm patterns.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Automata Theory Basics (DFA, NFA)
nodes.label: Automata Theory Basics (DFA, NFA)
nodes.excerpt: Automata theory studies abstract machines that recognize patterns in strings. Deterministic and nondeterministic finite automata model regular languages and provide the theory behind many regex and parser ideas.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/B-Trees and B+ Trees
nodes.label: B-Trees and B+ Trees
nodes.excerpt: B-trees and B+ trees are balanced multiway search trees optimized for storage systems. They reduce disk and page reads by keeping many keys per node and shallow tree height.

nodes.label: Backtracking
nodes.excerpt: Backtracking explores possible choices depth-first and abandons branches that cannot lead to valid solutions. It is useful for permutations, combinations, constraint satisfaction, puzzles, and exhaustive search with pruning.

nodes.label: Bit Manipulation Tricks
nodes.excerpt: Bit manipulation uses binary operations to represent sets, flags, masks, parity, and low-level arithmetic efficiently. Common tricks rely on AND, OR, XOR, shifts, complements, and two's-complement behavior.

nodes.label: Bloom Filters
nodes.excerpt: Bloom filters are compact probabilistic data structures for membership checks. They can say an item is definitely not present or possibly present, trading false positives for memory efficiency.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Computational Complexity Classes (P, NP, NP-Hard)
nodes.label: Computational Complexity Classes (P, NP, NP-Hard)
nodes.excerpt: Computational complexity classes group problems by the resources needed to solve or verify them. P, NP, and NP-hard are central categories for reasoning about algorithmic tractability.

nodes.label: Divide and Conquer
nodes.excerpt: Divide and conquer solves problems by splitting them into smaller subproblems, solving those recursively, and combining the results. Merge sort, quicksort, binary search, and many tree algorithms use this pattern.

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Dynamic programming solves problems with overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure by storing reusable results. Common patterns include one-dimensional DP, grid DP, knapsack, interval DP, tree DP, and state-machine DP.

nodes.label: Graph Traversal (BFS, DFS)
nodes.excerpt: Graph traversal systematically visits nodes and edges. Breadth-first search explores by distance layers, while depth-first search explores as far as possible before backtracking.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Graphs — Representations (Adjacency List vs Matrix)
nodes.label: Graphs — Representations (Adjacency List vs Matrix)
nodes.excerpt: Graph representation determines how efficiently algorithms inspect neighbors, edges, and connectivity. Adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs, while adjacency matrices make edge lookup simple but cost more memory.

nodes.label: Greedy Algorithms
nodes.excerpt: Greedy algorithms make the locally best choice at each step and hope it leads to a global optimum. They are fast and elegant when a proof shows the greedy choice is safe.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Hash Tables — Collision Resolution (Chaining, Open Addressing)
nodes.label: Hash Tables — Collision Resolution (Chaining, Open Addressing)
nodes.excerpt: Hash tables map keys to values using a hash function and an array of buckets. Collision resolution strategies such as chaining and open addressing determine how the table behaves when multiple keys land in the same place.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Heaps and Priority Queues
nodes.label: Heaps and Priority Queues
nodes.excerpt: Heaps are tree-shaped structures that efficiently expose the minimum or maximum element. Priority queues use heaps to process items by priority, supporting scheduling, shortest paths, streaming top-k, and event simulation.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
nodes.label: Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
nodes.excerpt: Linked lists store elements as nodes connected by pointers rather than contiguous memory. They make local insertion and deletion cheap when the node is known, but sacrifice cache locality and fast random access.

nodes.label: Memoization vs Tabulation
nodes.excerpt: Memoization and tabulation are two dynamic programming implementation styles. Memoization caches recursive calls top-down, while tabulation fills a table bottom-up in dependency order.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Trees (Kruskal, Prim)
nodes.label: Minimum Spanning Trees (Kruskal, Prim)
nodes.excerpt: Minimum spanning tree algorithms connect all vertices in a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge cost and no cycles. Kruskal and Prim solve the same problem using different greedy strategies.

nodes.label: NP-Completeness Overview
nodes.excerpt: NP-completeness identifies problems that are both in NP and at least as hard as every problem in NP. It explains why many problems appear to resist efficient exact algorithms.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Recursion and Stack Frames
nodes.label: Recursion and Stack Frames
nodes.excerpt: Recursion solves problems by having a function call itself on smaller inputs, with stack frames tracking each active call. Understanding base cases, call stacks, and return flow prevents infinite recursion and stack overflow.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Regular Expressions — Theory and Practice
nodes.label: Regular Expressions — Theory and Practice
nodes.excerpt: Regular expressions describe string patterns, originally corresponding to regular languages. Practical regex engines add features and tradeoffs that make regex powerful, useful, and sometimes dangerous for performance and readability.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Searching Algorithms (Binary Search and Variants)
nodes.label: Searching Algorithms (Binary Search and Variants)
nodes.excerpt: Searching algorithms locate items or boundaries in a data space. Binary search and its variants exploit monotonic structure to cut the search space in half repeatedly.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.label: Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.excerpt: Segment trees and Fenwick trees answer range queries while supporting updates efficiently. They are specialized data structures for sums, minimums, maximums, frequencies, and prefix-based calculations.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.label: Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.excerpt: Shortest path algorithms find low-cost routes through graphs. Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*, and Floyd-Warshall differ in edge-weight assumptions, negative-weight support, heuristics, and all-pairs versus single-source scope.

nodes.label: Skip Lists
nodes.excerpt: Skip lists maintain sorted order using layered linked lists with randomized promotion. They provide expected logarithmic search, insertion, and deletion while staying simpler than many balanced tree implementations.

nodes.label: Sliding Window Technique
nodes.excerpt: The sliding window technique maintains a contiguous range over an array or string while expanding and shrinking boundaries. It is useful for substring, subarray, frequency, and running-aggregate problems.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.excerpt: Comparison-based sorting orders items using pairwise comparisons. Merge sort, quicksort, heap sort, and Timsort differ in stability, memory use, worst-case behavior, cache behavior, and real-world performance.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.excerpt: Non-comparison sorting algorithms use assumptions about keys instead of only pairwise comparisons. Radix, counting, and bucket sort can beat comparison-sort bounds when value ranges, digit structure, or distribution assumptions are favorable.

nodes.label: Stacks and Queues
nodes.excerpt: Stacks and queues are linear data structures with constrained access patterns. Stacks use last-in-first-out behavior, while queues use first-in-first-out behavior, making them useful for parsing, traversal, scheduling, and buffering.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.label: String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.excerpt: String matching algorithms find occurrences of a pattern inside text. KMP uses prefix structure to avoid backing up, while Rabin-Karp uses rolling hashes to compare candidate windows efficiently.

nodes.label: Topological Sort
nodes.excerpt: Topological sort orders nodes in a directed acyclic graph so every dependency comes before the thing that depends on it. It is useful for build systems, course schedules, task planning, and dependency resolution.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.label: Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.excerpt: Trees are hierarchical data structures made of nodes and parent-child relationships. Binary trees, search trees, AVL trees, and red-black trees trade structure and balancing rules for efficient lookup, insertion, deletion, and ordered traversal.

nodes.label: Tries (Prefix Trees)
nodes.excerpt: Tries store strings by sharing common prefixes across paths in a tree. They are useful for autocomplete, prefix queries, dictionaries, routing tables, and problems where prefix structure matters more than full-key hashing.

nodes.label: Two-Pointer Technique
nodes.excerpt: The two-pointer technique uses two indices or references that move through data in coordinated ways. It is common for sorted arrays, linked lists, pair searches, partitioning, and cycle detection.

nodes.label: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
nodes.excerpt: Union-Find, or Disjoint Set Union, tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. Path compression and union by rank make repeated union and find operations nearly constant time in practice.

nodes.label: Caching
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: cache layers, read/write strategies, eviction policies, stampede/penetration/avalanche problems, Redis internals.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.label: Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol metabolism primarily converts ethanol to acetaldehyde and then acetate, mostly in the liver. Acetaldehyde is toxic, and metabolism affects sleep, inflammation, liver stress, cancer risk, and hangover symptoms.

nodes.label: Biochemistry — Proteins and Enzymes
nodes.excerpt: Proteins are amino-acid polymers that fold into functional structures, and enzymes are proteins or RNA molecules that catalyze reactions. Protein shape, binding sites, and environmental conditions determine function.

nodes.label: Caffeine Mechanism (Adenosine Antagonism)
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine mainly works by blocking adenosine receptors, reducing the feeling of sleep pressure and increasing alertness. It does not erase sleep debt, and its half-life makes timing important.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.label: Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.excerpt: Chemical bonding explains how atoms and molecules stick together through electron sharing, electron transfer, polarity, and weak intermolecular forces. Ionic, covalent, hydrogen, and van der Waals interactions shape chemistry and biology.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.label: DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.excerpt: DNA stores genetic information in a double-stranded nucleotide sequence, while RNA helps copy, regulate, and express that information. DNA, RNA, and proteins form the core information flow of biology.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.label: How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.excerpt: Brain-active drugs change neural signaling by interacting with receptors, transporters, enzymes, and ion channels. Effects depend on target, dose, timing, brain region, tolerance, individual biology, and context.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.label: Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.excerpt: Metabolic pathways transform nutrients into usable energy and cellular building blocks. Glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and the electron transport chain are central to ATP production.

nodes.label: Nootropics — Evidence-Based Overview
nodes.excerpt: Nootropics are substances marketed or used to improve cognition, but evidence, safety, regulation, and effect size vary widely. Sleep, exercise, treatment of real disorders, caffeine, and creatine have stronger grounding than most supplement stacks.

nodes.label: Organic Chemistry Basics
nodes.excerpt: Organic chemistry studies carbon-containing molecules, functional groups, structure, reactivity, and mechanisms. It matters because proteins, fats, carbohydrates, DNA, drugs, and many materials are organic chemistry in action.

nodes.label: Periodic Table Essentials
nodes.excerpt: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, electron structure, and recurring chemical properties. Its rows and columns reveal patterns in reactivity, bonding, size, electronegativity, and metallic character.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.label: Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.excerpt: Pharmacology studies how drugs interact with the body through absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, receptors, dose-response, efficacy, potency, and side effects. Effects depend on both molecule and context.

nodes.label: Protein Synthesis (Transcription and Translation)
nodes.excerpt: Protein synthesis turns genetic information into proteins through transcription and translation. DNA is transcribed into RNA, then ribosomes translate mRNA codons into amino-acid chains.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.label: The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep chemistry involves timing signals like melatonin, sleep-pressure signals like adenosine, inhibitory signaling through GABA, and many interacting systems. No single chemical explains sleep by itself.

nodes.label: Claude Code Skills
nodes.excerpt: how Claude Code skills work: markdown files injected into context, gstack architecture (templates + Bun build), gbrain per-project learnings, how to write your own skill.

nodes.label: Cloud Cost Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot, right-sizing, Graviton, auto scaling patterns, S3 lifecycle, NAT Gateway mitigation, data transfer costs, tagging, Cost Explorer.

nodes.label: Cloud Networking
nodes.excerpt: VPC, subnets, IGW, NAT Gateway, security groups, VPC peering (non-transitive), Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke), VPN vs Direct Connect, PrivateLink, data transfer pricing.

nodes.label: Cloud Services
nodes.excerpt: AWS and GCP services: EC2/GCE, Lambda/Cloud Functions, S3/GCS, EBS, RDS/Aurora, DynamoDB/Firestore, ElastiCache, SQS/SNS/Pub-Sub, ECS/EKS/GKE/Cloud Run, CloudFront, ALB/NLB, IAM, CloudWatch.

nodes.label: Anchoring Bias
nodes.excerpt: Anchoring bias occurs when an initial number, frame, or example pulls later judgments toward it. It affects negotiation, estimation, prioritization, design discussions, and planning.

nodes.label: Availability Heuristic
nodes.excerpt: The availability heuristic estimates likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind. Vivid, recent, emotional, or repeated examples can make rare events feel common or common events feel invisible.

nodes.label: Baader-Meinhof Phenomenon (Frequency Illusion)
nodes.excerpt: The Baader-Meinhof phenomenon, or frequency illusion, happens when something newly noticed suddenly seems everywhere. Attention and memory make the pattern more visible, not necessarily more frequent.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive biases are systematic tendencies in perception, memory, attention, and judgment that can distort reasoning. They are not personal defects; they are predictable shortcuts that need awareness, feedback, and better decision processes.

nodes.label: Cognitive Load Theory
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive load theory explains learning in terms of limited working memory and schema formation. Good instruction reduces unnecessary load, manages complexity, and helps learners build durable mental structures.

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias
nodes.excerpt: Confirmation bias is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember evidence that supports existing beliefs. It is especially dangerous when identity, ego, or sunk cost is attached to being right.

nodes.label: Decision Fatigue
nodes.excerpt: Decision fatigue is the decline in decision quality after repeated cognitive effort, uncertainty, or self-control demands. Reducing low-value choices preserves attention for decisions that matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.label: Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.excerpt: Dual process theory distinguishes fast, automatic, intuitive processing from slower, effortful, analytical processing. The System 1/System 2 framing is useful, but simplified and should not be treated as literal separate brains.

nodes.label: Dunning-Kruger Effect
nodes.excerpt: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how low skill can impair the ability to accurately judge skill, leading to overconfidence. The broader lesson is that calibration depends on feedback and domain knowledge.

nodes.label: Fundamental Attribution Error
nodes.excerpt: Fundamental attribution error is overemphasizing personality and underemphasizing situation when explaining other people's behavior. It can make mistakes look like character flaws instead of context, incentives, or constraints.

nodes.label: Heuristics in Decision-Making
nodes.excerpt: Heuristics are mental shortcuts that make decisions faster under uncertainty. They are useful when matched to the environment and dangerous when mistaken for complete analysis.

nodes.label: In-Group - Out-Group Bias
nodes.excerpt: In-group and out-group bias makes people favor their own group and interpret outsiders more negatively. It shapes trust, conflict, hiring, communities, politics, and team dynamics.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.label: Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Deductive reasoning derives necessary conclusions from premises, inductive reasoning generalizes from observations, and abductive reasoning infers the best explanation. Good problem-solving often uses all three.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.label: Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.excerpt: Logical fallacies are flawed argument patterns that weaken reasoning, such as straw man, ad hominem, false dilemma, circular reasoning, slippery slope, and appeal to authority. They are useful for analysis, but can be misused as debate weapons.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.label: Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.excerpt: Loss aversion is the tendency for losses to feel more psychologically powerful than equivalent gains. In prospect theory, people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point rather than pure final wealth.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.label: Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are reusable thinking tools for explaining systems, predicting outcomes, and making decisions. A strong model library includes technical, psychological, economic, probabilistic, and systems models.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.label: Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.excerpt: Nudge theory studies how choice architecture changes behavior without forbidding options. Defaults, friction, ordering, reminders, and framing can guide choices, for good or manipulative ends.

nodes.label: Optimism Bias
nodes.excerpt: Optimism bias is the tendency to overestimate positive outcomes and underestimate negative ones for oneself or one's plan. It can support motivation, but needs risk checks when decisions are expensive.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.label: Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Overfitting in human reasoning is building an explanation too tightly around limited examples, noise, or recent experience. The result feels insightful but fails to generalize.

nodes.label: Planning Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The planning fallacy is underestimating how long tasks will take despite knowing similar tasks have taken longer before. The outside view, buffers, and historical data are the main antidotes.

nodes.label: Recency Bias
nodes.excerpt: Recency bias overweights recent information relative to older but still relevant evidence. It affects hiring, investing, debugging, sports judgment, product decisions, and self-evaluation.

nodes.label: Status Quo Bias
nodes.excerpt: Status quo bias is the preference for existing conditions simply because they are familiar or default. It can protect stability, but also preserves outdated systems, habits, and assumptions.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.label: Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.excerpt: The streetlight effect is looking for answers where measurement is easiest rather than where the answer is most likely. It shows up in analytics, debugging, research, and productivity metrics.

nodes.label: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing a choice because of past investment rather than future value. Money, time, effort, identity, and public commitment can all make quitting feel irrationally hard.

nodes.label: Survivorship Bias
nodes.excerpt: Survivorship bias happens when analysis focuses only on examples that made it through a selection process. It distorts lessons from startups, careers, investing, fitness, history, and engineering incidents.

nodes.label: DSA Practice
nodes.excerpt: DSA patterns by category (arrays, trees, graphs, DP, backtracking, binary search) with spaced repetition notes.

nodes.label: Data Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ELT vs ETL, data lake (Parquet, Iceberg, Medallion architecture), dbt, Airflow/Dagster, Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift, Spark, Flink, Kinesis, Glue, CDC/Debezium, reverse ETL.

nodes.label: A-B Testing Design
nodes.excerpt: A/B testing compares product or system variants using randomized assignment and statistical analysis. Good test design defines the hypothesis, unit of randomization, metrics, sample size, guardrails, and stopping rules before launch.

nodes.label: Anomaly Detection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Anomaly detection finds observations or patterns that differ meaningfully from expected behavior. Useful methods range from simple thresholds to statistical baselines, clustering, isolation forests, and time-series models.

nodes.label: Bayesian Statistics
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian statistics treats probability as a degree of belief updated by evidence. It combines prior beliefs with observed data to produce posterior beliefs.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.label: Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.excerpt: Causal inference asks whether an action or exposure caused an outcome, not merely whether two variables moved together. It uses experiments, assumptions, controls, and quasi-experimental methods to reason about cause and effect.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.label: Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.excerpt: Clustering groups unlabeled data points by similarity. K-means, DBSCAN, and hierarchical clustering each make different assumptions about cluster shape, density, and how many groups exist.

nodes.label: Cohort Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Cohort analysis groups users or entities by a shared start condition and tracks behavior over time. It is especially useful for retention, activation, lifecycle, and product-quality questions.

nodes.label: Confidence Intervals
nodes.excerpt: Confidence intervals estimate a plausible range for an unknown population value from sample data. They communicate uncertainty better than a single point estimate, but they are often misread as probabilities about one fixed interval.

nodes.label: Dashboard Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Dashboard design organizes metrics so users can monitor status, diagnose changes, and act. Effective dashboards prioritize audience, metric definitions, hierarchy, freshness, comparisons, and drill-down paths.

nodes.label: Data Cleaning Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Data cleaning turns messy, inconsistent, duplicated, or invalid data into a reliable analysis dataset. It is less glamorous than modeling but often determines whether conclusions are trustworthy.

nodes.label: Data Lake Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Data lake architecture stores raw and processed data in scalable object storage for analytics, ML, and archival use. Its value depends on metadata, governance, file formats, partitioning, and transformation discipline.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.label: Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.excerpt: Data normalization and scaling transform numeric features into comparable ranges or distributions. They are essential for distance-based models, gradient-based optimization, and stable feature interpretation.

nodes.label: Data Warehousing Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Data warehousing organizes cleaned, queryable data for analytics and reporting. Core concepts include facts, dimensions, star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, marts, lineage, and governance.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.label: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.excerpt: Dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions for visualization, denoising, or modeling. PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP differ in whether they preserve global variance, local neighborhoods, or manifold structure.

nodes.label: ETL vs ELT
nodes.excerpt: ETL transforms data before loading it into a target store, while ELT loads raw data first and transforms it inside the warehouse or lakehouse. The choice affects governance, cost, flexibility, and pipeline ownership.

nodes.label: Feature Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Feature engineering turns raw data into model inputs that expose useful signal. It often matters more than model choice because features encode domain knowledge, time context, aggregations, and constraints.

nodes.label: Feature Selection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Feature selection chooses which variables to keep for modeling. It improves interpretability, reduces noise, lowers cost, and can prevent overfitting when done with proper validation.

nodes.label: Funnel Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Funnel analysis measures how users move through a sequence of steps toward a goal. It identifies drop-off points, conversion rates, and where product or process changes may matter most.

nodes.label: Handling Missing Data
nodes.excerpt: Handling missing data means understanding why values are absent and choosing a treatment that preserves valid inference. Deletion, imputation, missingness indicators, and model-aware methods each carry assumptions.

nodes.label: Hypothesis Testing
nodes.excerpt: Hypothesis testing evaluates whether observed data is surprising under a null hypothesis. It is useful for experiments, but it depends heavily on assumptions, sample size, and careful interpretation.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.label: Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.excerpt: Choosing metrics and KPIs means deciding what behavior the team should optimize and monitor. Strong metrics are aligned with goals, hard to game, clearly defined, and paired with guardrails.

nodes.label: OLAP vs OLTP
nodes.excerpt: OLTP systems handle live transactions, while OLAP systems support analytical queries over large historical datasets. Confusing the two leads to slow apps, expensive queries, and unreliable reporting.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.label: P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.excerpt: P-values measure how surprising observed data would be under a null hypothesis. Statistical significance is a thresholding convention, not proof that an effect is real, large, or important.

nodes.label: Probability Theory Basics
nodes.excerpt: Probability theory models uncertainty using events, random variables, distributions, expectation, and conditional probability. It underpins statistics, machine learning, experimentation, and risk reasoning.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.label: Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.excerpt: Regression analysis models relationships between inputs and an outcome. Linear, logistic, and polynomial regression are foundational tools for prediction, explanation, and estimating effects with assumptions.

nodes.label: SQL Window Functions
nodes.excerpt: SQL window functions compute values across related rows without collapsing the result set. They are essential for rankings, running totals, moving averages, retention analysis, and event-sequence queries.

nodes.label: SQL for Analytics
nodes.excerpt: SQL for analytics uses relational queries to answer business, product, and operational questions. The practical skill is joining, filtering, grouping, aggregating, and validating data without losing the meaning of the metric.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.label: Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.excerpt: Statistics fundamentals summarize data with measures of center and spread. Mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation are the starting vocabulary for reasoning about datasets.

nodes.label: Survival Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Survival analysis models time until an event occurs, such as churn, failure, conversion, or relapse. It handles censored data where the event has not happened yet during the observation window.

nodes.label: Time Series Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Time series analysis studies data ordered over time, such as metrics, demand, latency, or prices. It focuses on trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, forecasting, and anomaly detection.

nodes.label: Visualization Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Visualization best practices help charts communicate patterns without distorting the data. Good visualizations choose the right chart type, scale, labeling, ordering, color, and context for the question.

nodes.label: dbt (Data Build Tool)
nodes.excerpt: dbt is a transformation tool that lets analytics teams build versioned, tested SQL models in the warehouse. It brings software engineering practices such as modularity, lineage, documentation, and CI to analytics work.

nodes.label: Data Structures and Algorithms Patterns
nodes.excerpt: LeetCode/NeetCode pattern guide covering arrays, hash maps, two pointers, sliding windows, heaps, graphs, union find, backtracking, DP, and tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Internals Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper database internals: pages, WAL, MVCC, vacuum/bloat, indexes, query planner, isolation anomalies, locks, replication, backups, partitioning, distributed SQL.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.label: Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Database backups are only useful if they can be restored, and point-in-time recovery uses backups plus logs to recover to a specific moment. Recovery planning should be tested, not assumed.

nodes.label: Database Change Data Capture (CDC)
nodes.excerpt: Change Data Capture streams database changes into downstream systems without requiring every application path to publish events manually. CDC is useful for analytics, search indexing, cache updates, replication, and event-driven integration.

nodes.label: Database Connection Pooling (pgBouncer, SQLAlchemy)
nodes.excerpt: Connection pooling reuses database connections so applications do not open expensive new sessions for every request. Tools like pgBouncer and SQLAlchemy pools protect the database from connection storms and improve latency.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.label: Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.excerpt: Database indexes are access structures tuned for different query shapes. B-tree indexes cover common equality and range queries, while hash, GIN, and GiST indexes support more specialized lookup patterns.

nodes.label: Database Normalization (1NF through BCNF)
nodes.excerpt: Database normalization reduces redundancy and update anomalies by decomposing tables according to dependency rules. The practical point is preserving facts once, in the right place, unless performance demands a documented exception.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.label: Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.excerpt: Full-text search indexes terms, rankings, and language-aware matching so users can find documents by meaning-adjacent text instead of exact values. PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, and Typesense cover different complexity levels.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Document Stores (MongoDB)
nodes.excerpt: Document stores organize data as flexible JSON-like documents, often optimizing for aggregate-oriented reads and schema evolution. MongoDB is the common reference point, but document databases still require intentional modeling.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Graph Databases (Neo4j)
nodes.excerpt: Graph databases model data as nodes, edges, and properties, making relationship traversal a first-class operation. They are strongest when the relationships are the product, not just supporting metadata.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Key-Value Stores (Redis)
nodes.excerpt: Key-value stores map keys directly to values and optimize for simple, fast lookups. Redis is the common example, often used for caching, counters, locks, queues, pub/sub, and rate limiting.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Wide-Column Stores (Cassandra)
nodes.excerpt: Wide-column stores like Cassandra organize data by partition keys and clustering columns for massive distributed writes and reads at known access patterns. They reward query-first modeling and punish ad hoc querying.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.label: ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.excerpt: ORMs map database tables to application objects, improving ergonomics but sometimes hiding query cost. The N+1 problem, lazy loading surprises, and leaky abstractions are the classic failure modes.

nodes.label: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking
nodes.excerpt: Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare and checks before commit, while pessimistic locking blocks concurrent writers earlier. The right choice depends on contention, user experience, and correctness requirements.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.label: PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.excerpt: PostgreSQL goes beyond classic relational storage with JSONB, arrays, full-text search, extensions, custom types, and strong indexing. Its strength is being a reliable relational core that can absorb many adjacent workloads.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.label: Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.excerpt: Redis is commonly used as a cache, pub/sub broker, lightweight queue, distributed coordination tool, counter store, and rate limiter. The main engineering risk is treating every Redis pattern as equally durable or reliable.

nodes.label: Relational Database Design
nodes.excerpt: Relational database design models data as tables, rows, relationships, keys, and constraints. Good schema design makes invariants explicit so application code is not the only thing protecting correctness.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.label: SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.excerpt: Indexes give databases faster access paths, while query plans show how the optimizer intends to execute SQL. EXPLAIN turns performance work from guessing into inspection.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.label: SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.excerpt: SQL joins combine rows across tables using relationship predicates. Understanding inner, outer, and cross joins is essential for reading query intent and avoiding accidental row multiplication.

nodes.label: SQL — Subqueries and CTEs
nodes.excerpt: Subqueries and common table expressions break SQL logic into nested or named steps. They improve readability when used well, but can also hide expensive intermediate work.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.label: SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.excerpt: SQL transactions group work so it commits or rolls back as one unit. ACID captures atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability: the core guarantees behind reliable relational state changes.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.label: SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.excerpt: SQLite is an embedded relational database stored in a local file. It is excellent for local apps, prototypes, edge deployments, tests, and small services, but it is not a drop-in replacement for client-server databases under high write concurrency.

nodes.label: Time Series Databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
nodes.excerpt: Time series databases optimize for timestamped measurements, retention policies, downsampling, and range queries over time. They fit metrics, IoT readings, financial ticks, logs, and operational telemetry.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: ACID, MVCC, indexes (B-tree/hash/composite/covering), query planning, N+1, connection pooling, SQL vs all 4 NoSQL types, PostgreSQL specifics.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.label: AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.excerpt: AWS core services map to common infrastructure primitives: EC2 for virtual machines, S3 for object storage, Lambda for functions, RDS for managed relational databases, and VPC for private networking. These services form the base vocabulary for many AWS architectures.

nodes.label: Ansible Basics
nodes.excerpt: Ansible automates configuration management, provisioning, and operational tasks over SSH or connection plugins. It is useful for making server state repeatable without requiring an agent on every machine.

nodes.label: Bash Scripting
nodes.excerpt: Bash scripting automates command-line workflows by composing programs, variables, conditionals, loops, functions, and exit codes. Good scripts are boring, explicit, quoted carefully, and written with failure behavior in mind.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.label: Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Blue-green, canary, and rolling deployments are rollout strategies for reducing release risk. They differ in infrastructure cost, speed, rollback behavior, traffic control, and how quickly user impact is detected.

nodes.label: CI-CD Pipeline Design
nodes.excerpt: CI/CD pipeline design automates building, testing, packaging, securing, and deploying software. A good pipeline is fast enough for developer feedback, strict enough to protect production, and observable enough to debug failures.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.label: Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.excerpt: AWS, GCP, and Azure provide managed compute, storage, networking, database, security, and analytics services. Choosing among them is usually about ecosystem fit, team familiarity, enterprise constraints, pricing shape, and managed-service maturity.

nodes.label: Cloudflare Services Overview
nodes.excerpt: Cloudflare provides edge services for DNS, CDN caching, DDoS mitigation, TLS, web application security, access control, workers, tunnels, and traffic routing. It is often used as the public edge in front of applications.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.label: Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.excerpt: Cron jobs and task schedulers run recurring work such as reports, cleanup, backups, imports, and maintenance. Reliable scheduling requires idempotency, locking, observability, retries, time-zone care, and clear ownership.

nodes.label: Database Migrations Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Database migration best practices let teams change schemas and data safely while applications keep running. The safest migrations are small, backward-compatible, observable, reversible where possible, and sequenced with application deployments.

nodes.label: Disk I-O Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Disk I/O optimization reduces latency and throughput bottlenecks in reads, writes, fsyncs, logs, databases, and storage systems. It requires understanding access patterns, caching, batching, indexes, queues, and storage-device limits.

nodes.label: Error Budgets
nodes.excerpt: Error budgets convert an SLO into the amount of unreliability a service can tolerate during a time window. They help teams balance feature delivery against reliability work with a concrete shared threshold.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: GitHub Actions is a workflow automation platform built around events, jobs, steps, runners, actions, secrets, and permissions. Using it well means controlling triggers, caches, credentials, artifacts, and least-privilege access.

nodes.label: GitOps Principles
nodes.excerpt: GitOps manages infrastructure and application delivery by storing desired state in Git and reconciling runtime systems to match it. It improves auditability, review, rollback, and drift detection when repository boundaries and permissions are designed well.

nodes.label: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
nodes.excerpt: Infrastructure as Code manages cloud and platform resources through versioned declarative or scripted definitions. It makes infrastructure reviewable, repeatable, auditable, and safer to change.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.label: Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes YAML defines desired state for workloads, networking, and routing through objects such as Deployments, Services, and Ingresses. Reading these manifests means understanding selectors, pods, replicas, ports, health checks, and controller behavior.

nodes.label: Lambda Functions and FaaS
nodes.excerpt: Lambda functions and Function-as-a-Service platforms run short-lived code in response to events. They are useful for bursty event processing, API handlers, automation, and glue logic when limits and retry behavior are designed explicitly.

nodes.label: Linux Command Line Essentials
nodes.excerpt: Linux command line essentials cover navigating files, inspecting processes, managing text streams, checking resources, editing permissions, and debugging services. Fluency at the shell is still one of the highest-leverage infrastructure skills.

nodes.label: Log Aggregation (ELK Stack, Loki)
nodes.excerpt: Log aggregation centralizes application and infrastructure logs so engineers can search, correlate, alert, and debug across machines and services. ELK and Loki represent two common approaches to storing and querying logs.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.label: Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.excerpt: Operating-system memory management gives processes isolated virtual address spaces backed by physical memory, page tables, caches, and swap. Understanding pages, page faults, OOM behavior, and memory pressure helps debug production systems.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.label: Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.excerpt: Observability uses logs, metrics, and traces to understand system behavior from the outside. The goal is not collecting more telemetry, but making production failures diagnosable and service health measurable.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.label: On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.excerpt: On-call culture and incident response define how teams detect, triage, mitigate, communicate, and learn from production failures. Healthy on-call systems protect users without burning out engineers.

nodes.label: OpenTelemetry
nodes.excerpt: OpenTelemetry is an open standard and toolkit for collecting traces, metrics, and logs across services. It helps teams avoid vendor-specific instrumentation while preserving context propagation and consistent telemetry.

nodes.label: Performance Profiling (CPU, Memory, I-O)
nodes.excerpt: Performance profiling identifies where CPU, memory, I/O, network, or lock contention is limiting a system. Good profiling starts from symptoms and measurements before optimizing code or infrastructure.

nodes.label: Postmortem Writing
nodes.excerpt: Postmortem writing turns incidents into shared learning and concrete prevention work. A strong postmortem records impact, timeline, contributing factors, detection gaps, response quality, and follow-up actions without blame.

nodes.label: Prometheus and Grafana
nodes.excerpt: Prometheus collects time-series metrics and Grafana visualizes them in dashboards and alerts. Together they are a common open-source stack for monitoring service health, latency, errors, saturation, and infrastructure behavior.

nodes.label: Reverse Proxies (Nginx, Caddy, Traefik)
nodes.excerpt: Reverse proxies sit in front of backend services to route traffic, terminate TLS, balance load, enforce policies, and add observability. Nginx, Caddy, and Traefik represent different tradeoffs in control, automation, and dynamic configuration.

nodes.label: Runbooks and Playbooks
nodes.excerpt: Runbooks and playbooks document repeatable operational procedures for diagnosing and resolving known situations. Good ones are concise, current, executable, and linked to alerts or service ownership.

nodes.label: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs
nodes.excerpt: SLIs measure service behavior, SLOs set reliability targets, and SLAs define external commitments and consequences. Together they translate reliability from vague uptime goals into measurable engineering decisions.

nodes.label: SSH Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: SSH best practices reduce the risk of unauthorized server access while preserving secure administrative workflows. Key ideas include key-based auth, least privilege, bastions, agent hygiene, host verification, logging, and avoiding shared credentials.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.label: Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.excerpt: Secret management stores, distributes, rotates, and audits credentials such as API keys, database passwords, certificates, and tokens. Tools like Vault and AWS Secrets Manager reduce hardcoded secrets but still require careful IAM, rotation, and runtime access design.

nodes.label: Serverless Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Serverless architecture uses managed services where the provider handles server provisioning, scaling, and much of the operations surface. It can reduce infrastructure toil, but shifts design concerns to events, limits, cold starts, observability, cost shape, and vendor boundaries.

nodes.label: Terraform Basics
nodes.excerpt: Terraform is a declarative Infrastructure as Code tool that plans and applies changes to cloud and platform resources. Its core model is providers, resources, modules, state, variables, outputs, and plan/apply workflows.

nodes.label: VPN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: VPN architecture creates encrypted network connectivity between users, devices, offices, clouds, or private networks. Good VPN design considers identity, routing, split tunneling, access scope, device trust, performance, and whether zero-trust access is a better fit.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.label: systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.excerpt: systemd manages Linux services, process supervision, dependencies, logs, timers, and boot-time units. Understanding unit files, service lifecycle, journald, and restart behavior is essential for operating Linux servers.

nodes.label: Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: partial failures, CAP/PACELC, consistency models, replication (single-leader/multi-leader/leaderless), Raft consensus, 2PC vs Saga, vector clocks, gossip protocol.

nodes.label: Docker
nodes.excerpt: OverlayFS layer system, Dockerfile best practices (multi-stage builds), networking (bridge/host), volumes, container runtime architecture (dockerd→containerd→runc→kernel).

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: focused DP guide covering state design, memoization vs tabulation, 1D/2D DP, knapsack, LIS, interval DP, tree DP, bitmask DP, common traps, and practice method.

nodes.label: Economics and Personal Finance
nodes.excerpt: practical economics and personal finance primer covering incentives, opportunity cost, supply/demand, compounding, emergency funds, debt, index funds, retirement accounts, taxes, credit, lifestyle inflation, and career finance.

nodes.label: Engineering Judgment and Tool Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: practical guide to choosing tools by fit, failure modes, accepted tradeoffs, monitoring signals, and simpler alternatives.

nodes.label: FlashAttention
nodes.excerpt: GPU memory hierarchy, why standard attention is HBM-bound, tiling algorithm, online softmax, FA-2 and FA-3.

nodes.label: Formal Methods Agent Safety
nodes.excerpt: temporal logic specs, type systems, runtime monitors, NN verification (and its limits), probabilistic verification, neurosymbolic approaches.

nodes.label: Geospatial
nodes.excerpt: geohashing (8-neighbor fix), quadtrees, S2/Hilbert curve, Haversine formula, Yelp-like proximity design, Uber-like live driver matching with Redis GEOSEARCH, H3 hexagons, map tile systems.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.label: Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.excerpt: Back health for desk workers depends on movement variety, strength, load tolerance, sleep, stress, and workstation setup. Sitting is not automatically harmful, but staying rigid and inactive for long periods can be.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.label: Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.excerpt: Blood work can reveal health patterns that subjective feeling misses, but markers need clinical context. Commonly useful areas include lipids, glucose control, blood count, thyroid, liver, kidney, iron, vitamin D, and inflammation markers.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.label: Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.excerpt: Breathing techniques use deliberate breath patterns to influence arousal, attention, and perceived stress. Box breathing, the physiological sigh, and Wim Hof-style breathing differ in intensity and should be matched to context and safety.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.label: Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.excerpt: Cardio methods differ by intensity and adaptation: HIIT trains high-intensity capacity, LISS builds easy aerobic volume, and Zone 2 targets sustainable aerobic base. The right mix depends on goals, recovery, and preference.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.label: Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.excerpt: Cold exposure may help alertness, perceived recovery, and cold tolerance, but evidence for broad health claims is still limited and risks are real. Protocols should be conservative, brief, and avoided by people with relevant cardiovascular or medical risk unless cleared.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.label: Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.excerpt: Compound lifts train multiple joints and muscle groups at once, making them efficient strength and muscle-building movements. Squat, deadlift, bench press, and overhead press are common anchors but not mandatory for every person.

nodes.label: Deload Weeks
nodes.excerpt: A deload week intentionally reduces training stress to dissipate fatigue while preserving momentum. Deloads are useful when fatigue accumulates, performance stalls, or a program has run hard for several weeks.

nodes.label: Exercise Science Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Exercise science studies how the body adapts to training stress through strength, endurance, mobility, recovery, and energy systems. Useful programming balances overload, specificity, fatigue, and consistency.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.label: Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.excerpt: Eye strain from screen work often comes from sustained near focus, glare, dryness, poor lighting, and long uninterrupted sessions. The 20-20-20 rule is a simple break habit: every 20 minutes, look 20 feet away for about 20 seconds.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.label: Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.excerpt: Fasting protocols can support calorie control or metabolic goals for some people, but they may conflict with training performance, recovery, medication timing, or disordered eating risk. The protocol should match the goal and individual context.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.label: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.excerpt: Heart rate variability reflects variation between heartbeats and is often used as a recovery and stress signal. It is useful as a trend, not a single-day verdict, and should be interpreted alongside sleep, mood, performance, and illness.

nodes.label: Hypertrophy vs Strength Programming
nodes.excerpt: Hypertrophy programming prioritizes muscle growth, while strength programming prioritizes force output in specific movements. They overlap, but differ in volume, intensity, exercise selection, and progression.

nodes.label: Mobility and Flexibility Training
