nodes.excerpt: Mobility is active control through range of motion, while flexibility is the available passive range. Good training combines strength, control, and appropriate stretching rather than chasing range alone.

nodes.label: NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)
nodes.excerpt: NEAT is the energy used for non-exercise movement like walking, standing, chores, fidgeting, and daily activity. It can meaningfully affect energy expenditure and health without feeling like formal workouts.

nodes.label: Overtraining vs Overreaching
nodes.excerpt: Overreaching is short-term accumulated fatigue that can be recovered from, while overtraining is a more serious maladaptive state from prolonged stress and insufficient recovery. The distinction matters for programming and health.

nodes.label: Posture and Desk Ergonomics
nodes.excerpt: Desk ergonomics reduces strain by matching monitor, chair, keyboard, mouse, lighting, and break habits to the body. The best posture is variable: comfort comes from movement and setup, not holding one perfect position.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.label: Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Recovery is the process that turns training stress into adaptation. Sleep, nutrition, hydration, low-intensity movement, stress management, and appropriate programming all determine whether work becomes progress.

nodes.label: Sauna — Evidence and Use
nodes.excerpt: Sauna bathing is associated with cardiovascular and relaxation benefits, but much evidence is observational and individual safety matters. It should be treated as a heat-stress recovery tool, not a cure-all.

nodes.label: Step Count and NEAT
nodes.excerpt: Step count is a practical proxy for everyday movement and NEAT. It supports health, energy expenditure, and desk-work recovery without requiring every activity to become structured exercise.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.label: Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.excerpt: Strength training improves force production by progressively challenging muscles and nervous system patterns. The core principles are overload, specificity, sufficient volume, technical consistency, and recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.label: Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.excerpt: Static, dynamic, and PNF stretching use different methods to improve range of motion or prepare for activity. Dynamic stretching fits warmups, static stretching fits flexibility work, and PNF uses contraction-relaxation patterns.

nodes.label: Sunlight Exposure and Health
nodes.excerpt: Sunlight supports circadian rhythm, mood, and vitamin D synthesis, but ultraviolet exposure also increases skin cancer and eye-disease risk. The practical balance is morning light, outdoor time, and sun protection when UV exposure is significant.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.label: VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.excerpt: VO2 max measures the maximum rate of oxygen use during intense exercise and is a strong marker of aerobic fitness. It improves through consistent aerobic training, intervals, and enough recovery.

nodes.label: Wrist Health for Programmers
nodes.excerpt: Wrist health for programmers depends on input ergonomics, varied movement, reasonable workload, and early response to discomfort. The goal is reducing repetitive strain before it becomes persistent pain.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.label: Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Zone 2 cardio is sustainable aerobic work that improves mitochondrial function, fat oxidation, endurance base, and recovery capacity. It should feel controlled enough to maintain for a long duration.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.label: Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.excerpt: Bell Labs was one of the most productive industrial research labs in history, contributing the transistor, information theory, Unix, C, lasers, cellular concepts, and major physics discoveries. It shows what long-horizon research institutions can produce.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.label: History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.excerpt: AI history moves through symbolic AI, expert systems, AI winters, statistical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and foundation models. The field repeatedly cycles between ambition, disappointment, new methods, and scale.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Cloud Computing
nodes.label: History of Cloud Computing
nodes.excerpt: Cloud computing evolved from time-sharing, virtualization, hosting, and utility computing into on-demand infrastructure, platforms, serverless, managed databases, and global developer services. AWS popularized the modern cloud model.

nodes.label: History of Cryptography
nodes.excerpt: Cryptography evolved from hand ciphers and wartime codebreaking into public-key systems, internet security, digital signatures, cryptocurrencies, and privacy-preserving protocols. Its history tracks the tension between secrecy, trust, commerce, and state power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Mobile Computing
nodes.label: History of Mobile Computing
nodes.excerpt: Mobile computing developed through laptops, PDAs, cellular networks, BlackBerry-style messaging, smartphones, app stores, and sensor-rich always-connected devices. It changed software around location, cameras, notifications, and constant presence.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Open Source
nodes.label: History of Open Source
nodes.excerpt: Open source grew from academic and hacker sharing cultures into a dominant software production model. It includes free software ideals, pragmatic open-source licensing, Linux, Apache, Git, GitHub, and modern dependency ecosystems.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.label: History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.excerpt: Personal computing moved computers from institutions to individuals through hobbyist kits, Apple, IBM PCs, GUIs, laptops, the internet, and smartphones. The iPhone completed a shift toward always-connected pocket computing.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Programming Languages
nodes.label: History of Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: Programming languages evolved from machine code and assembly into high-level, structured, object-oriented, functional, scripting, systems, and domain-specific languages. Each generation traded control, safety, expressiveness, performance, and developer speed differently.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Search Engines
nodes.label: History of Search Engines
nodes.excerpt: Search engines evolved from human directories and early crawlers into ranking systems, ad platforms, vertical search, semantic search, and AI-assisted retrieval. The key shift was from indexing pages to ranking relevance at web scale.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Social Media
nodes.label: History of Social Media
nodes.excerpt: Social media evolved from forums, blogs, and early networks into algorithmic feeds, creator economies, messaging platforms, and attention markets. Its history is also a history of identity, virality, moderation, and surveillance advertising.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.label: History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.excerpt: Video game console history moves through arcade-inspired home systems, the 1983 crash, Nintendo's revival, 3D graphics, optical media, online services, motion controls, and hybrid hardware. Consoles are shaped by hardware cycles, exclusive games, distribution, and living-room culture.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Games
nodes.label: History of Video Games
nodes.excerpt: Video game history runs from arcade experiments and home consoles through PC gaming, 3D graphics, online play, mobile games, esports, streaming, and live-service economies. It is both technology history and culture history.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.label: History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.excerpt: The internet evolved from packet-switching research and ARPANET into a global network built on TCP/IP, DNS, email, the web, broadband, mobile, cloud, and newer decentralization claims. Its history is a story of open protocols, institutions, commercialization, and power shifts.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.label: History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.excerpt: The transistor replaced vacuum tubes and made modern electronics possible, while the integrated circuit packed many components onto one chip. Together they enabled computing, consumer electronics, telecommunications, and Moore's Law scaling.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.label: History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.excerpt: The web evolved from linked documents into interactive platforms, social applications, cloud-backed SaaS, and contested Web3 decentralization narratives. Its history is distinct from the internet: the web is one application layer built on internet protocols.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.label: Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.excerpt: Moore's Law described the repeated doubling of transistor density, driving decades of cheaper and faster computing. Its slowing has shifted progress toward specialization, parallelism, chiplets, accelerators, and software efficiency.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.label: Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.excerpt: The smartphone rose from earlier mobile phones, PDAs, BlackBerry, and touch devices, but the iPhone crystallized a new model: multitouch, full web, sensors, app ecosystem, and pocket computing as daily infrastructure.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.label: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.excerpt: The age of surveillance capitalism describes business models that extract, predict, and shape behavior through data collection and targeted advertising. The concept is associated with Shoshana Zuboff and helps explain platform incentives.

nodes.label: The Browser Wars
nodes.excerpt: The browser wars were battles over the web's main user interface, from Netscape vs Internet Explorer to later competition involving Firefox, Chrome, Safari, and Edge. They shaped standards, performance, security, and platform power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.label: The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.excerpt: The dotcom boom and bust was a late-1990s internet investment bubble followed by a crash, but the underlying infrastructure and user behavior kept growing. It is a classic case of real technology plus unsustainable speculation.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.label: The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.excerpt: The Homebrew Computer Club was a Silicon Valley hobbyist community that helped launch personal computing culture. It connected hardware tinkerers, software enthusiasts, and early entrepreneurs, including Apple founders.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.label: The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.excerpt: The open source movement blends free software ideals with practical collaborative development. GNU, Linux, Apache, BSD, and later GitHub made open infrastructure central to modern computing.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.label: Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.excerpt: Xerox PARC pioneered or refined key personal-computing ideas: graphical interfaces, Ethernet, laser printing, object-oriented environments, WYSIWYG editing, and networked workstations. Its story is also a lesson in invention versus commercialization.

nodes.label: IaaS PaaS SaaS
nodes.excerpt: service model spectrum: IaaS (raw VMs), PaaS (managed runtime), SaaS (fully managed app), FaaS (functions), and where the lines blur.

nodes.label: Interview Communication
nodes.excerpt: interview communication guide covering project explanations, STAR stories, coding/system design structure, debugging out loud, clarifying questions, recovery, and resume bullet framing.

nodes.label: Kubernetes
nodes.excerpt: desired state reconciliation, control plane, worker nodes, all core objects (Pod/Deployment/Service/Ingress/StatefulSet/HPA/PV), scheduling, health probes, networking (CNI/kube-proxy/CoreDNS), RBAC.

nodes.label: LLM Capabilities and AI Ethics
nodes.excerpt: opinionated overview of what LLMs can and cannot do, their tradeoffs, whether they replace engineers/humans, and the philosophy, morality, and ethics of AI deployment.

nodes.label: LLM System Design
nodes.excerpt: tokens, context windows, prompt caching (KV cache, TTL, cost math), streaming, tool use/agentic loops, RAG (chunking, hybrid search, reranking), multi-agent orchestration, evals, fine-tuning vs prompting.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.label: Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.excerpt: Active recall strengthens memory by forcing retrieval, while passive review mainly creates familiarity. Durable learning comes from trying to remember before looking at the answer.

nodes.label: Anki — Optimal Card Design
nodes.excerpt: Good Anki cards test one clear idea, use active recall, and minimize ambiguity. Card quality matters more than deck size because vague cards train frustration instead of memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.label: Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.excerpt: Calibration is the skill of matching confidence to reality. It improves through prediction, feedback, decision journaling, and noticing where understanding feels fluent but fails under retrieval or application.

nodes.label: Chunking Information
nodes.excerpt: Chunking groups smaller pieces of information into meaningful units so working memory can handle them more efficiently. Expertise often looks like larger and better-organized chunks.

nodes.label: Deliberate Practice (Anders Ericsson)
nodes.excerpt: Deliberate practice is focused practice designed to improve a specific weakness with feedback, challenge, and repetition. It differs from simply spending time because it targets the edge of current ability.

nodes.label: Desirable Difficulties in Learning
nodes.excerpt: Desirable difficulties are learning conditions that feel harder in the short term but improve long-term retention and transfer. Examples include spacing, retrieval, interleaving, variation, and generation.

nodes.label: First Principles Thinking
nodes.excerpt: First principles thinking breaks a problem down to basic truths and rebuilds from there. It is useful when analogies, conventions, or inherited assumptions are constraining the solution.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.label: Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.excerpt: Growth mindset frames ability as improvable through effort, strategy, and feedback, while fixed mindset treats ability as static. The useful version is not empty positivity; it is choosing better practice after failure.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.label: How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.excerpt: Memory involves encoding information, storing it over time, and retrieving it later. Learning improves when attention, meaning, retrieval practice, spacing, and context are designed deliberately.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.label: How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.excerpt: Reading a technical paper is an active process: identify the question, contribution, method, assumptions, evidence, limitations, and relevance before getting lost in details. Multiple passes usually beat one slow linear read.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Retain What You Read
nodes.label: How to Retain What You Read
nodes.excerpt: Retaining what you read requires active processing: previewing, questioning, summarizing, recalling, connecting, and applying. Highlighting alone creates familiarity but weak long-term recall.

nodes.label: Interleaving Practice
nodes.excerpt: Interleaving practice mixes related problem types instead of drilling one type in a block. It improves discrimination because learners must decide which strategy applies, not just repeat the obvious one.

nodes.label: Inversion Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Inversion thinking solves problems by asking what would cause failure, then avoiding or counteracting those causes. It is useful because failure modes are often easier to identify than perfect solutions.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a new domain fast means building a map of core concepts, vocabulary, constraints, experts, tools, and feedback loops. The fastest path combines overview, targeted projects, active recall, and conversations with knowledgeable people.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a programming language fast means focusing on syntax, type system, runtime model, package workflow, idioms, and small real programs. The fastest route is comparing it to languages already known while building something concrete.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.label: Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are reusable representations for understanding how something works or what matters in a situation. They improve judgment when they are accurate, connected, and tested against reality.

nodes.label: Metacognition
nodes.excerpt: Metacognition is thinking about one's own thinking: planning, monitoring, and evaluating learning or decision processes. It helps learners choose strategies intentionally instead of confusing effort with progress.

nodes.label: Note-Taking Methods (Cornell, Outline, Zettelkasten)
nodes.excerpt: Note-taking methods differ in how they structure attention, recall, and synthesis. Cornell supports study review, outlines support hierarchy, and Zettelkasten supports connected long-term thinking.

nodes.label: Productive Disagreement
nodes.excerpt: Productive disagreement turns conflict into better understanding by separating claims, evidence, assumptions, and values. It works when participants try to improve the model rather than win the exchange.

nodes.label: Second-Order Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Second-order thinking looks beyond the immediate outcome to the downstream consequences of a decision. It is useful for avoiding fixes that solve today's problem by creating tomorrow's.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.label: Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.excerpt: Spaced repetition schedules reviews just before forgetting so memories are strengthened efficiently. It works best for durable facts, concepts, prompts, and procedures that benefit from repeated retrieval.

nodes.label: Systems Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Systems thinking studies relationships, feedback loops, delays, incentives, and boundaries rather than isolated parts. It is useful for understanding why local fixes can fail in complex environments.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.label: Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.excerpt: Teaching strengthens learning because it forces retrieval, organization, simplification, and feedback. Explaining a topic clearly reveals missing links that passive review can hide.

nodes.label: The Feynman Technique
nodes.excerpt: The Feynman Technique tests understanding by explaining a concept simply, finding gaps, revisiting the source, and refining the explanation. It is useful because clear explanation exposes fuzzy knowledge.

nodes.label: The Learning Pyramid
nodes.excerpt: The Learning Pyramid is a popular but oversimplified model claiming different retention rates by activity. Its useful takeaway is that active methods often beat passive review, but the exact percentages should not be treated as evidence.

nodes.label: Transfer of Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer of learning is applying knowledge or skill from one context to another. It improves when learners understand underlying principles, practice varied examples, and compare cases instead of memorizing surface patterns.

nodes.label: MLOps and ML Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ML lifecycle from data and labels through training, experiment tracking, model registry, deployment, monitoring, drift, retraining, and LLMOps for Bobby-like systems.

nodes.label: Bayes' Theorem
nodes.excerpt: Bayes' theorem updates belief in a hypothesis after observing evidence. It relates prior probability, likelihood, evidence probability, and posterior probability.

nodes.label: Big-O Notation and Complexity Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Big-O notation describes how algorithm cost grows as input size increases. Complexity analysis helps compare approaches by time, space, and scalability rather than by machine-specific timing.

nodes.label: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates
nodes.excerpt: Boolean algebra reasons about true/false values with operations such as AND, OR, NOT, XOR, NAND, and NOR. Logic gates implement these operations in hardware and shape digital circuits, conditionals, filters, and bit manipulation.

nodes.label: Calculus — Derivatives and Integrals
nodes.excerpt: Calculus studies change and accumulation through derivatives and integrals. Derivatives measure local rate of change, while integrals measure accumulated quantity over an interval.

nodes.label: Central Limit Theorem
nodes.excerpt: The Central Limit Theorem says that sums or averages of many independent random variables tend toward a normal distribution under broad conditions. It explains why normal approximations appear so often in statistics.

nodes.label: Chain Rule (in Context of Backprop)
nodes.excerpt: The chain rule computes derivatives of composed functions by multiplying local derivative effects. Backpropagation applies the chain rule through a neural network so each parameter receives its contribution to the loss.

nodes.label: Combinatorics Basics (Permutations, Combinations)
nodes.excerpt: Combinatorics counts arrangements and selections. Permutations care about order, combinations do not, and both are foundational for probability, algorithm analysis, search spaces, and counting arguments.

nodes.label: Discrete Mathematics for CS
nodes.excerpt: Discrete mathematics studies countable structures such as sets, logic, graphs, integers, relations, functions, and combinatorics. It is the mathematical foundation for algorithms, data structures, databases, cryptography, and formal reasoning.

nodes.label: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
nodes.excerpt: Eigenvectors are directions that a linear transformation only scales, and eigenvalues are the scaling factors. They reveal hidden structure in matrices and appear in PCA, graph analysis, differential equations, and stability reasoning.

nodes.label: Expected Value and Variance
nodes.excerpt: Expected value is the long-run average outcome of a random variable, while variance measures how spread out outcomes are around that average. Together they summarize central tendency and uncertainty.

nodes.label: Fourier Transform Intuition
nodes.excerpt: The Fourier transform represents signals as combinations of frequencies instead of values over time or space. It is useful for audio, images, compression, filtering, signal processing, and understanding periodic structure.

nodes.label: Gradient and Partial Derivatives
nodes.excerpt: Partial derivatives measure how a multivariable function changes with respect to one input, and the gradient collects those derivatives into a vector. Gradients drive optimization by pointing toward steepest local increase.

nodes.label: Graph Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Graph theory studies nodes and edges, making it the natural language for networks, dependencies, maps, social systems, state spaces, and many algorithms. Core ideas include directedness, weights, paths, cycles, connectivity, trees, and traversal.

nodes.id: Resources/Mathematics/Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.label: Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.excerpt: Information theory measures uncertainty, surprise, and difference between probability distributions. Entropy, cross-entropy, and KL divergence are central to compression, machine learning losses, language models, and variational inference.

nodes.label: Lagrange Multipliers
nodes.excerpt: Lagrange multipliers solve constrained optimization problems by combining an objective with constraint equations. At an optimum, the objective gradient aligns with constraint gradients under regularity conditions.

nodes.label: Law of Large Numbers
nodes.excerpt: The Law of Large Numbers says sample averages converge toward expected value as sample size grows. It explains why repeated measurement and large samples reduce random noise, while not eliminating bias.

nodes.label: Linear Algebra — Vectors and Matrices
nodes.excerpt: Linear algebra studies vectors, matrices, linear transformations, and systems of equations. It is the mathematical language behind graphics, machine learning, optimization, embeddings, and many engineering models.

nodes.label: Markov Chains
nodes.excerpt: Markov chains model systems that move between states where the next state depends only on the current state. They are useful for queues, random walks, language modeling history, simulations, ranking, and stochastic processes.

nodes.label: Matrix Multiplication and Dot Products
nodes.excerpt: Dot products measure alignment between vectors, while matrix multiplication composes linear transformations and combines rows with columns. These operations power projections, neural network layers, graphics transforms, and similarity search.

nodes.label: Monte Carlo Methods
nodes.excerpt: Monte Carlo methods use random sampling to estimate quantities that may be hard to compute exactly. They are useful for simulation, integration, uncertainty estimation, randomized algorithms, and probabilistic inference.

nodes.label: Multivariable Calculus Basics
nodes.excerpt: Multivariable calculus extends calculus to functions with multiple inputs or outputs. It introduces partial derivatives, gradients, Jacobians, Hessians, and constrained optimization.

nodes.label: Number Theory Basics (Primes, Modular Arithmetic)
nodes.excerpt: Number theory studies integers, divisibility, primes, congruences, and modular arithmetic. It underlies hashing, cryptography, randomized algorithms, checksums, and many programming puzzle patterns.

nodes.label: Optimization — Convex vs Non-Convex
nodes.excerpt: Optimization searches for inputs or parameters that minimize or maximize an objective. Convex optimization has nicer global guarantees, while non-convex optimization can contain local minima, saddle points, and difficult landscapes.

nodes.label: Probability Distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Exponential)
nodes.excerpt: Probability distributions describe how likely different outcomes are. Normal, binomial, Poisson, and exponential distributions model common patterns such as measurement noise, success counts, event counts, and waiting times.

nodes.label: Set Theory
nodes.excerpt: Set theory studies collections of distinct objects and operations such as union, intersection, difference, complement, and membership. It is a basic language for logic, databases, probability, type systems, and discrete math.

nodes.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
nodes.excerpt: reverse-engineering transformers: circuit hypothesis, induction heads, IOI circuit, superposition, sparse autoencoders, activation patching.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.label: ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.excerpt: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition involving attention, impulsivity, executive function, and sometimes hyperactivity. Management can include diagnosis, medication, therapy, coaching, environmental design, sleep, and external structure.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.label: Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.excerpt: Anxiety is a threat-response state that becomes a disorder when it is excessive, persistent, impairing, or mismatched to reality. Coping tools include breathing, exposure, cognitive work, sleep, exercise, support, and professional care when needed.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.label: Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.excerpt: Attachment styles describe patterns of closeness, trust, threat, and regulation in relationships. Secure, anxious, avoidant, and fearful patterns are useful lenses, but they are not fixed identities or full explanations of a person.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.label: Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Burnout is commonly understood as exhaustion, cynicism or detachment, and reduced efficacy from chronic unmanaged stress, especially work stress. Recovery usually requires workload changes, boundaries, rest, support, and meaning repair.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.label: Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.excerpt: Chronic stress is repeated or sustained demand without enough recovery. It can affect mood, sleep, attention, immune function, cardiovascular risk, appetite, pain sensitivity, and relationships.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.label: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy links thoughts, emotions, behaviors, and body sensations, then uses structured practice to change unhelpful patterns. It is evidence-based for many conditions, but therapy should be delivered by qualified clinicians when treating disorders.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive distortions are recurring biased thought patterns that can intensify anxiety, depression, shame, and conflict. Naming them helps create distance from the thought so it can be tested rather than automatically believed.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.label: Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.excerpt: Depression is a serious mood disorder involving emotion, cognition, sleep, appetite, energy, motivation, and body systems. Treatment can include psychotherapy, medication, lifestyle support, social support, and sometimes advanced interventions under clinical care.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.label: Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.excerpt: The dopamine system is involved in motivation, reward prediction, learning, salience, and movement. It is not simply a pleasure chemical; it helps the brain learn what is worth pursuing.

nodes.label: Emotional Regulation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Emotional regulation means noticing, naming, tolerating, and responding to emotions without being controlled by them. Techniques include breathing, grounding, reappraisal, movement, journaling, problem-solving, and seeking support.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.label: FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.excerpt: FOMO and digital anxiety arise when feeds, notifications, social comparison, and constant availability make missing information feel costly. The fix is not knowing everything, but setting boundaries around inputs and values.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.label: Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.excerpt: Grief is a varied response to loss, not a fixed five-stage checklist. Modern grief research emphasizes oscillation, meaning, continuing bonds, individual differences, and support rather than one universal timeline.

nodes.label: Impostor Syndrome
nodes.excerpt: Impostor syndrome is the persistent feeling of being a fraud despite evidence of competence. It often appears in high-achieving or uncertain environments and improves through evidence tracking, normalization, feedback, and realistic standards.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.label: Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.excerpt: Journaling supports mental health by externalizing thoughts, naming emotions, tracking patterns, and turning vague distress into clearer problems. It works best when it leads to reflection or action rather than rumination loops.

nodes.label: Learned Helplessness
nodes.excerpt: Learned helplessness is a pattern where repeated uncontrollable setbacks teach someone that action will not matter. Recovery often involves restoring small reliable links between effort, choice, feedback, and outcome.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.label: Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.excerpt: Locus of control describes whether someone tends to see outcomes as shaped by their own actions or by external forces. A healthy internal locus supports agency, while realism keeps it from becoming self-blame.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.label: Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.excerpt: Loneliness is the felt gap between desired and actual connection, and it is associated with worse mental and physical health. Connection is a health behavior, not just a nice extra.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.label: Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Meditation includes practices such as mindfulness, focused attention, open monitoring, loving-kindness, body scan, mantra, and movement meditation. Benefits depend on the person, practice, consistency, and psychological context.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.label: Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.excerpt: Mindfulness is paying attention to present experience with less automatic judgment. Evidence supports it as a helpful skill for stress and emotion regulation for many people, though it is not a universal cure.

nodes.label: Neuroplasticity
nodes.excerpt: Neuroplasticity is the nervous system's ability to change through learning, experience, injury, and practice. It supports growth and recovery, but it is gradual and constrained, not a magic reset button.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.label: Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.excerpt: Perfectionism combines high standards with fear, rigidity, or self-worth dependence. It improves when standards become flexible, feedback becomes information, and imperfect action becomes safer than avoidance.

nodes.label: Post-Traumatic Growth
nodes.excerpt: Post-traumatic growth describes positive psychological change that some people report after trauma, such as deeper relationships, changed priorities, or increased meaning. It should never be used to romanticize trauma or pressure people to find benefits.

nodes.label: Psychological Safety
nodes.excerpt: Psychological safety is the shared belief that people can speak up, ask questions, admit mistakes, and challenge ideas without interpersonal punishment. It is crucial for learning teams and honest engineering work.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.label: Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.excerpt: Resilience is the capacity to adapt, recover, and keep functioning under adversity. It is built through relationships, recovery, meaning, flexible thinking, skills, health basics, and exposure to manageable challenges.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.label: Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.excerpt: Self-compassion means responding to difficulty with kindness, common humanity, and mindful awareness, while self-esteem evaluates worth or competence. Kristin Neff's framing is useful because self-compassion supports resilience without needing to feel superior or successful.

nodes.label: Serotonin System
nodes.excerpt: Serotonin is involved in mood, sleep, appetite, pain, gut function, and many brain-body processes. It is not simply the happiness chemical, and depression cannot be reduced to low serotonin alone.

nodes.label: Social Comparison Theory
nodes.excerpt: Social comparison theory says people evaluate themselves by comparing to others, especially under uncertainty. Comparison can inform learning, but it can also distort self-worth, motivation, and mood.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.label: Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.excerpt: Applied Stoicism focuses on what is within one's control, responding to events with virtue and judgment, and practicing perspective before reaction. Its modern value is emotional discipline without emotional suppression.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.label: Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.excerpt: The stress response coordinates nervous-system and hormonal changes through systems including the HPA axis and cortisol. Acute stress can be adaptive, but chronic activation can harm sleep, mood, immunity, metabolism, and decision-making.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.label: The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.excerpt: Play in adult life supports exploration, bonding, stress relief, creativity, learning, and identity. It is not wasted time when it restores energy, connection, and flexible thinking.

nodes.label: Message Queues
nodes.excerpt: delivery guarantees (at-most/at-least/exactly-once), Kafka internals (partitions, offsets, consumer groups, zero-copy, EOS), SQS, RabbitMQ, outbox pattern, event sourcing, backpressure.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent RL
nodes.excerpt: MARL: non-stationarity, CTDE, QMIX monotonicity, MAPPO, self-play, league play, emergent communication, credit assignment.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.excerpt: multi-agent architectures (orchestrator-worker, blackboard, peer-to-peer), emergent behaviors, failure modes, safety and control.

nodes.label: Networking
nodes.excerpt: OSI model, IP/NAT/CIDR, TCP internals (handshake, sliding window, congestion control, BBR), UDP, DNS resolution, TLS 1.3 (Diffie-Hellman, forward secrecy, mTLS), HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 (QUIC).

nodes.label: Amygdala and Emotional Processing
nodes.excerpt: The amygdala helps detect emotionally salient information, especially potential threat, and coordinates responses with memory, attention, and body systems. It is not simply the fear center.

nodes.label: Attention Networks in the Brain
nodes.excerpt: Attention depends on interacting brain networks that support alertness, orienting, executive control, salience detection, and internally focused thought. Focus is not one switch; it is a dynamic allocation system.

nodes.label: Brain Plasticity in Adults
nodes.excerpt: Adult brains remain plastic through learning, practice, therapy, injury recovery, and environment, but plasticity is constrained by biology, attention, repetition, sleep, and context. Change is possible without being effortless.

nodes.label: Brain Regions and Functions (Simplified Map)
nodes.excerpt: A simplified brain map links regions to common functions, but real cognition comes from distributed networks. Useful regions include prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hippocampus, basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, and sensory cortices.

nodes.label: Default Mode Network
nodes.excerpt: The default mode network is a set of brain regions active during self-referential thought, mind-wandering, autobiographical memory, and simulation. It is not bad by default, but can be involved in rumination.

nodes.label: Hippocampus and Memory Formation
nodes.excerpt: The hippocampus is central for forming episodic memories and linking experiences to context, time, and place. It supports memory consolidation with broader cortical networks.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/How Exercise Affects the Brain (BDNF, Neurogenesis)
nodes.label: How Exercise Affects the Brain (BDNF, Neurogenesis)
nodes.excerpt: Exercise affects the brain through blood flow, neurotrophic factors like BDNF, mood regulation, sleep, insulin sensitivity, inflammation, and possibly hippocampal neurogenesis. Human claims should be careful because mechanisms are easier to show in animals than in people.

nodes.label: How Sleep Consolidates Memory
nodes.excerpt: Sleep supports memory consolidation by replaying, stabilizing, and integrating learning across neural systems. Deep NREM and REM appear to support different aspects of memory, emotion, and abstraction.

nodes.label: Interoception — Body Awareness and Emotions
nodes.excerpt: Interoception is the brain's sensing and interpretation of internal body signals such as heartbeat, breath, hunger, temperature, pain, and gut sensations. It shapes emotion, anxiety, self-awareness, and regulation.

nodes.label: Long-Term Potentiation (LTP) and Memory Formation
nodes.excerpt: Long-term potentiation is a lasting increase in synaptic strength after specific activity patterns. It is one major cellular model for learning and memory, especially in hippocampal circuits.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/Neurochemistry Overview (Dopamine, Serotonin, GABA, Glutamate, Norepinephrine)
nodes.label: Neurochemistry Overview (Dopamine, Serotonin, GABA, Glutamate, Norepinephrine)
nodes.excerpt: Neurochemistry studies signaling molecules that shape mood, attention, learning, arousal, inhibition, and movement. Dopamine, serotonin, GABA, glutamate, and norepinephrine are major players, but none maps cleanly to one emotion or behavior.

nodes.label: Neuron Structure and Electrochemical Signaling
nodes.excerpt: Neurons are specialized cells that receive, integrate, and transmit information through electrical impulses and chemical signaling. Their structure includes dendrites, soma, axon, myelin, terminals, and synapses.

nodes.label: Prefrontal Cortex and Decision-Making
nodes.excerpt: The prefrontal cortex supports planning, inhibition, working memory, goal maintenance, and decision-making. It is especially important when behavior must follow long-term goals rather than immediate impulses.

nodes.label: Psychedelics and Neuroplasticity (Current Research)
nodes.excerpt: Current psychedelic research suggests compounds like psilocybin can acutely alter brain network dynamics and may promote plasticity-related processes, often through serotonin 5-HT2A pathways. The field is promising but early, regulated, and not suitable for casual self-treatment framing.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — Neurons that Fire Together)
nodes.label: Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — Neurons that Fire Together)
nodes.excerpt: Synaptic plasticity is the ability of synapses to strengthen or weaken based on activity. Hebbian learning is the simplified idea that connections strengthen when neurons are active together.

nodes.label: The Gut-Brain Connection (Enteric Nervous System)
nodes.excerpt: The gut-brain connection involves the enteric nervous system, vagus nerve, immune signals, hormones, microbial metabolites, and nutrient sensing. It is real biology, but many consumer claims outrun what can be personalized today.

nodes.label: Notification System Design
nodes.excerpt: End-to-end notification system design covering aggregation, two-tier pipelines, reliable delivery, the outbox pattern, and a comparison against Meta's production system.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Alcohol and Health — What the Research Says
nodes.label: Alcohol and Health — What the Research Says
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol affects sleep, liver health, cancer risk, accidents, mood, and performance, and current public-health framing increasingly emphasizes that lower intake means lower risk. Any benefits are context-dependent and should not be treated as a reason to drink.

nodes.label: Anti-Inflammatory Foods
nodes.excerpt: Anti-inflammatory eating usually means a pattern rich in minimally processed plants, fiber, unsaturated fats, fish, legumes, and adequate protein, not a single miracle food. The goal is a dietary pattern that supports metabolic and cardiovascular health.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Caffeine Timing and Adenosine Clearance
nodes.label: Caffeine Timing and Adenosine Clearance
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine timing matters because caffeine has a multi-hour half-life and blocks adenosine signals rather than erasing sleep pressure. Morning or early-day use is usually safer for sleep than afternoon or evening dosing.

nodes.label: Caffeine — Pharmacology and Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine is a stimulant that blocks adenosine receptors, increasing alertness and reducing perceived fatigue. Useful dosing depends on tolerance, sleep sensitivity, timing, anxiety, and performance needs.

nodes.label: Caloric Deficit and Surplus
nodes.excerpt: A caloric deficit generally drives weight loss, while a surplus supports weight gain. Body composition outcomes depend on protein, training, sleep, adherence, and the size of the deficit or surplus.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Creatine — Benefits, Safety, and Protocol
nodes.label: Creatine — Benefits, Safety, and Protocol
nodes.excerpt: Creatine monohydrate is one of the best-supported supplements for strength, power, and lean mass support. A simple daily dose is usually enough, and most healthy adults tolerate it well.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Glycemic Index and Blood Sugar Management
nodes.label: Glycemic Index and Blood Sugar Management
nodes.excerpt: Glycemic index ranks carbohydrate foods by blood-glucose response, but real meals depend on portion size, fiber, protein, fat, food form, and individual context. It is a tool, not a complete nutrition system.

nodes.label: Gut Microbiome Basics
nodes.excerpt: The gut microbiome is the community of microbes living in the digestive tract, influencing digestion, immune function, metabolites, and possibly mood. Diet diversity, fiber, fermented foods, sleep, stress, and antibiotics all affect it.

nodes.label: Gut-Brain Axis
nodes.excerpt: The gut-brain axis describes communication between the digestive system, microbiome, immune system, hormones, and nervous system. It is real biology, but consumer claims often run ahead of actionable evidence.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Hydration — How Much, When, and Electrolytes
nodes.label: Hydration — How Much, When, and Electrolytes
nodes.excerpt: Hydration needs depend on body size, sweat rate, climate, diet, activity, and health context. Water is the foundation, while electrolytes matter most with heavy sweating, long exercise, heat, illness, or low-sodium diets.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Intermittent Fasting — Methods and Evidence
nodes.label: Intermittent Fasting — Methods and Evidence
nodes.excerpt: Intermittent fasting restricts eating windows or days and may help some people reduce calorie intake or improve metabolic markers. Evidence does not make it universally superior, and safety depends on health status and context.

nodes.label: Macronutrients — Protein, Fat, Carbohydrates
nodes.excerpt: Macronutrients are the major energy-providing nutrients: protein, fat, and carbohydrates. Each has different roles in muscle repair, hormones, energy, satiety, performance, and overall diet quality.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Magnesium — Uses, Forms, and Deficiency
nodes.label: Magnesium — Uses, Forms, and Deficiency
nodes.excerpt: Magnesium supports muscle, nerve, energy, and enzymatic function. Forms differ in tolerability and use, and supplementation is most sensible when intake is low, symptoms or labs suggest deficiency, or a clinician recommends it.

nodes.label: Meal Timing and Performance
nodes.excerpt: Meal timing can affect training energy, recovery, digestion, and sleep, but total intake usually matters more for body composition. Timing is most important around hard training, long sessions, and personal tolerance.

nodes.label: Metabolic Rate and TDEE
nodes.excerpt: Metabolic rate and total daily energy expenditure estimate how much energy the body uses. TDEE includes resting metabolism, movement, exercise, digestion, and adaptive changes.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Micronutrients — Key Vitamins and Minerals
nodes.label: Micronutrients — Key Vitamins and Minerals
nodes.excerpt: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals needed in small amounts for metabolism, immunity, bone health, oxygen transport, and nervous system function. Deficiency risk depends on diet, health status, lifestyle, and medical context.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Omega-3 Fatty Acids — EPA, DHA, and Inflammation
nodes.label: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — EPA, DHA, and Inflammation
nodes.excerpt: Omega-3 fatty acids EPA and DHA are important fats found mainly in fatty fish and algae. They are associated with heart, brain, and inflammatory pathways, but supplement value depends on diet, dose, and health context.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Processed vs Whole Foods — What Matters
nodes.label: Processed vs Whole Foods — What Matters
nodes.excerpt: Whole foods tend to provide fiber, micronutrients, satiety, and lower energy density, while highly processed foods can be easier to overeat. The meaningful distinction is overall diet pattern, not moral labeling of individual foods.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Protein Intake for Muscle Building and Recovery
nodes.label: Protein Intake for Muscle Building and Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Protein intake supports muscle repair, hypertrophy, satiety, and recovery. For active people, daily total protein matters most, with distribution across meals helping muscle protein synthesis.

nodes.label: Supplements Worth Taking (Evidence-Based)
nodes.excerpt: Evidence-based supplements are context-dependent tools, not replacements for sleep, training, diet quality, or medical care. Creatine, caffeine, protein powder, vitamin D when low, omega-3 when intake is low, and electrolytes in specific conditions are common practical examples.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Vitamin D — Importance and Dosing
nodes.label: Vitamin D — Importance and Dosing
nodes.excerpt: Vitamin D supports bone health, immune function, and calcium regulation, with status influenced by sun exposure, diet, skin tone, latitude, season, and supplementation. Dosing is best guided by risk factors and blood levels when possible.

nodes.label: OS Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: processes (fork/exec/CoW), virtual memory (page tables, MMU, TLB), file descriptors, I/O models (epoll, io_uring), concurrency primitives, CFS scheduling, signals, namespaces, cgroups.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.excerpt: monitoring vs observability, structured logging, log levels, metrics (RED/USE methods, Prometheus, PromQL), distributed tracing (spans, context propagation, sampling), OpenTelemetry, SLOs/error budgets.

nodes.label: Observability Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: production debugging guide using logs, metrics, traces, SLOs, alerts, postmortems, and case studies for latency, queues, databases, and error spikes.

nodes.label: Annual Review Template
nodes.excerpt: An annual review reflects on what happened, what changed, what worked, what hurt, and what should be different next year. It is most useful when it turns reflection into priorities, constraints, and experiments.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.label: Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.excerpt: Atomic Habits argues that small repeated behaviors compound, and habit design should make good habits obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying. The identity angle matters: habits stick better when they reinforce who someone is becoming.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Building a Second Brain (BASB — Tiago Forte)
nodes.label: Building a Second Brain (BASB — Tiago Forte)
nodes.excerpt: Building a Second Brain is Tiago Forte's method for capturing, organizing, distilling, and expressing knowledge. Its practical value is making information reusable for projects instead of storing it as passive reference.

nodes.label: Capture Systems (Ubiquitous Capture)
nodes.excerpt: Ubiquitous capture means having a fast, trusted way to record ideas, tasks, commitments, and observations wherever they appear. Capture only works if the inbox is later clarified and processed.

nodes.label: Commonplace Book Tradition
nodes.excerpt: The commonplace book tradition collects quotations, observations, excerpts, and ideas for later reflection. Its modern value is not hoarding references, but giving interesting material a place to be revisited and connected.

nodes.label: Daily Note Template
nodes.excerpt: A daily note template gives each day a lightweight place for captures, priorities, reflections, and session logs. The best template is short enough to use consistently and structured enough to review later.

nodes.label: Decision Journaling
nodes.excerpt: Decision journaling records important decisions, assumptions, expected outcomes, confidence, and later results. It improves judgment by separating decision quality from outcome luck.

nodes.label: Deep Work (Cal Newport)
nodes.excerpt: Deep Work is Cal Newport's term for cognitively demanding, distraction-free work that creates high-value output. It requires protected time, clear goals, and deliberate resistance to shallow-task drift.

nodes.label: Digital Minimalism
nodes.excerpt: Digital minimalism is the deliberate use of technology based on values, not defaults. The aim is not rejecting tools, but removing low-value digital noise so attention goes to meaningful work and relationships.

nodes.label: Distraction Management
nodes.excerpt: Distraction management is the practice of shaping environment, devices, commitments, and work blocks so attention is less likely to be pulled away. It works best when it removes triggers rather than relying on willpower.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Energy Management vs Time Management
nodes.label: Energy Management vs Time Management
nodes.excerpt: Energy management asks whether a person has the physical, emotional, and cognitive capacity for the work scheduled. It complements time management because an empty calendar block is not useful if energy is mismatched.

nodes.label: Evening Wind-Down Routines
nodes.excerpt: An evening wind-down routine helps the brain transition out of work and into sleep or recovery. It works by lowering stimulation, closing open loops, and making the next morning easier.

nodes.label: Evergreen Notes
nodes.excerpt: Evergreen notes are durable, reusable notes that stay valuable beyond the day they were written. They work best when they express a clear idea in your own words and connect to related notes.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Flow State — How to Trigger It
nodes.label: Flow State — How to Trigger It
nodes.excerpt: Flow is a state of deep absorption where challenge and skill are well matched. It is more likely when goals are clear, feedback is immediate, distractions are low, and the task is neither too easy nor too overwhelming.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Focus Modes and Context Switching Costs
nodes.label: Focus Modes and Context Switching Costs
nodes.excerpt: Focus modes reduce context switching by grouping compatible work and protecting attention from unrelated inputs. Context switches are costly because the brain carries attention residue from the previous task.

nodes.label: Getting Things Done (GTD) Method
nodes.excerpt: Getting Things Done is a productivity method built around capturing every commitment, clarifying the next action, organizing by context, reviewing regularly, and trusting an external system instead of mental juggling.

nodes.label: Goal Frameworks (OKRs, SMART, 12-Week Year)
nodes.excerpt: Goal frameworks like OKRs, SMART goals, and the 12-Week Year make desired outcomes more concrete. They differ in emphasis, but all try to convert vague ambition into measurable focus and reviewable commitments.

nodes.label: Habit Stacking
nodes.excerpt: Habit stacking attaches a new behavior to an existing routine so the old habit becomes the cue. It works best when the new action is small, specific, and naturally follows the anchor behavior.

nodes.label: Inbox Zero Strategy
nodes.excerpt: Inbox Zero is not about having no messages forever; it is about regularly processing inboxes into decisions, actions, references, or deletion. The goal is reduced ambiguity, not obsessive cleanliness.

nodes.label: Intention Setting
nodes.excerpt: Intention setting clarifies what kind of action or attention a person wants to bring to a period of time. It is lighter than goal planning and useful for aligning behavior before work, conversations, or routines.

nodes.label: Keystone Habits
nodes.excerpt: Keystone habits are behaviors that trigger broader positive changes across routines, identity, and environment. They matter because one well-chosen habit can make many other habits easier.

nodes.label: Managing Information Overwhelm
nodes.excerpt: Managing information overwhelm means limiting inputs, clarifying what deserves action, and turning useful material into trusted systems. The goal is not consuming everything, but preserving attention for what matters.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Morning Routines — Research and Design
nodes.label: Morning Routines — Research and Design
nodes.excerpt: A morning routine should prepare energy, attention, and priorities for the day, not imitate someone else's ideal schedule. The best routine is small, repeatable, and matched to real constraints.

nodes.label: Motivation — Intrinsic vs Extrinsic
nodes.excerpt: Intrinsic motivation comes from interest, meaning, mastery, or identity, while extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards or consequences. Durable systems often combine both without letting rewards crowd out meaning.

nodes.label: Notification Hygiene
nodes.excerpt: Notification hygiene means deciding which alerts deserve immediate attention and silencing the rest. Good defaults protect deep work while preserving truly time-sensitive signals.

nodes.label: Obsidian Workflow Design
nodes.excerpt: Obsidian workflow design is about making capture, linking, review, and retrieval easy enough to sustain. For Kenny, the best workflow is discussion first, distilled notes second, and index updates so Bobby can actually use the vault.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/PARA Method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives)
nodes.label: PARA Method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives)
nodes.excerpt: PARA organizes information into Projects, Areas, Resources, and Archives based on actionability. It is useful because it separates active outcomes from ongoing responsibilities and reference material.

nodes.label: Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) Overview
nodes.excerpt: Personal Knowledge Management is the practice of capturing, organizing, connecting, and revisiting knowledge so it compounds over time. In this vault, PKM matters because Bobby relies on notes that are specific enough to retrieve and useful enough to act on.

nodes.label: Pomodoro Technique
nodes.excerpt: The Pomodoro Technique breaks work into focused intervals with short breaks, commonly 25 minutes on and 5 minutes off. It is most useful as an anti-procrastination and attention-starting tool.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Procrastination — Root Causes and Fixes
nodes.label: Procrastination — Root Causes and Fixes
nodes.excerpt: Procrastination is often emotion regulation, ambiguity, fear, low energy, or task design failure rather than laziness. Fixes work best when they target the specific cause.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Reward Systems and Dopamine Loops
nodes.label: Reward Systems and Dopamine Loops
nodes.excerpt: Reward systems shape behavior by reinforcing cues, actions, and outcomes. Dopamine is more about anticipation and learning than simple pleasure, which is why variable rewards and easy feedback loops can become sticky.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Task Management Philosophies (GTD, Autofocus, ZTD)
nodes.label: Task Management Philosophies (GTD, Autofocus, ZTD)
nodes.excerpt: Task management philosophies differ in how they capture, prioritize, review, and execute work. GTD emphasizes trusted capture and next actions, Autofocus emphasizes intuitive list scanning, and ZTD simplifies GTD into habits.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/The Map of Content (MOC) in Obsidian
nodes.label: The Map of Content (MOC) in Obsidian
nodes.excerpt: A Map of Content is a curated navigation note that organizes related notes around a topic, project, or area. In Obsidian, MOCs help turn dense link networks into intentional pathways.

nodes.label: Time Blocking
nodes.excerpt: Time blocking assigns work to calendar blocks so priorities compete against real time instead of an abstract task list. It is useful for protecting deep work and making tradeoffs visible.

nodes.label: Weekly Review Process
nodes.excerpt: A weekly review refreshes trusted systems by closing loops, reviewing commitments, updating priorities, and deciding what matters next. It prevents task systems and notes from slowly becoming stale.

nodes.label: Zettelkasten Method
nodes.excerpt: Zettelkasten is a linked-note method that emphasizes small, durable ideas connected through explicit relationships. Its value comes from turning notes into a thinking network rather than a pile of excerpts.

nodes.label: AI Ethics — Alignment and Safety
nodes.excerpt: AI ethics around alignment and safety asks how advanced systems can reliably pursue human-compatible goals, avoid harmful behavior, remain controllable, and be evaluated before deployment. It combines technical, governance, and moral questions.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/AI Ethics — Bias, Fairness, and Accountability
nodes.label: AI Ethics — Bias, Fairness, and Accountability
nodes.excerpt: AI bias, fairness, and accountability concern how automated systems can reproduce unequal data, incentives, and institutional power. Fairness is not one metric; it requires context, affected stakeholders, auditability, and responsibility.

nodes.label: Absurdism (Camus)
nodes.excerpt: Absurdism, associated with Albert Camus, begins from the conflict between human longing for meaning and an indifferent universe. Camus' answer is not despair, but revolt, freedom, and lucid living.

nodes.label: Effective Altruism — Ideas and Critiques
nodes.excerpt: Effective altruism applies evidence and reason to doing the most good, often through cause prioritization, cost-effectiveness, and long-term impact. Critiques focus on uncertainty, power, measurement, moral demandingness, and institutional failures.

nodes.label: Epistemology — How We Know Things
nodes.excerpt: Epistemology studies knowledge, belief, justification, evidence, truth, skepticism, and uncertainty. It matters because every serious reasoning system depends on how claims are supported and revised.

nodes.label: Ethics — Consequentialism
nodes.excerpt: Consequentialism judges actions by their outcomes. It is powerful for policy, engineering, and AI ethics, but struggles with prediction, rights, distribution, and whether good ends can justify harmful means.

nodes.label: Ethics — Deontology (Kant's Categorical Imperative)
nodes.excerpt: Deontology judges actions by duties, rules, rights, and respect for persons rather than outcomes alone. Kant's categorical imperative asks whether an action's principle could be universalized and whether people are treated as ends.

nodes.label: Ethics — Virtue Ethics (Aristotle)
nodes.excerpt: Virtue ethics focuses on character, practical wisdom, and becoming the kind of person who acts well. Aristotle frames virtues as trained dispositions aimed at human flourishing.

nodes.label: Existentialism Basics (Sartre, Camus, Heidegger)
nodes.excerpt: Existentialism focuses on freedom, responsibility, meaning, authenticity, anxiety, and the human condition. Sartre, Camus, and Heidegger differ, but all examine what it means to live without easy external guarantees.

nodes.label: Falsifiability and the Demarcation Problem
nodes.excerpt: Falsifiability is the idea that scientific claims should be testable in ways that could prove them wrong. The demarcation problem asks how to distinguish science from non-science or pseudoscience.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Free Will — Compatibilism vs Hard Determinism
nodes.label: Free Will — Compatibilism vs Hard Determinism
nodes.excerpt: Free will debates ask whether human choice is compatible with determinism, causation, and responsibility. Compatibilists redefine freedom around agency and reasons, while hard determinists deny ultimate free will.

nodes.label: Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers)
nodes.excerpt: David Chalmers' hard problem of consciousness asks why and how physical processes give rise to subjective experience. It differs from easier problems like explaining behavior, attention, reportability, or neural mechanisms.

nodes.label: Longtermism
nodes.excerpt: Longtermism is the view that positively influencing the long-run future is a major moral priority because future people may be vast in number. Its critiques focus on uncertainty, power, neglect of present harms, and speculative reasoning.

nodes.label: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
nodes.excerpt: Moral realism says moral truths exist independently of our attitudes, while moral anti-realism denies or reinterprets that independence. The debate shapes whether ethics is discovered, constructed, expressed, or negotiated.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Personal Identity Over Time (Ship of Theseus)
nodes.label: Personal Identity Over Time (Ship of Theseus)
nodes.excerpt: Personal identity over time asks what makes a person the same person despite physical, psychological, and memory changes. The Ship of Theseus captures the puzzle of continuity through replacement.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Language (Wittgenstein, Austin)
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of language studies meaning, reference, use, speech acts, and how words connect to the world. Wittgenstein emphasized language games, while Austin showed that saying things can also be doing things.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Mind — Consciousness
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of mind studies consciousness, mental states, identity, perception, intentionality, and the mind-body relationship. It asks how subjective experience relates to physical brains and behavior.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Science (Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos)
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of science studies how scientific knowledge is produced, justified, revised, and socially organized. Popper, Kuhn, and Lakatos offer different models of falsification, paradigms, and research programs.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Philosophy of Time — A-Theory vs B-Theory
nodes.label: Philosophy of Time — A-Theory vs B-Theory
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of time asks whether past, present, and future are objectively different or equally real. A-theory treats the present as special, while B-theory treats time as an ordered relation without objective passage.

nodes.label: Pragmatism (Peirce, James, Dewey)
nodes.excerpt: Pragmatism evaluates ideas through their practical consequences, inquiry, and usefulness in experience. Peirce, James, and Dewey differ, but all resist philosophy detached from action.

nodes.label: Rationalism vs Empiricism
nodes.excerpt: Rationalism emphasizes reason, innate structure, or a priori knowledge, while empiricism emphasizes sensory experience and observation. Modern inquiry usually needs both conceptual reasoning and empirical correction.

nodes.label: Solipsism and External World Skepticism
nodes.excerpt: Solipsism and external world skepticism question whether anything beyond one's own mind can be known. They are important as philosophical stress tests, but not usually livable or useful as everyday operating beliefs.

nodes.label: Stoicism vs Buddhism — Comparison
nodes.excerpt: Stoicism and Buddhism both train attention, desire, suffering, and response to impermanence, but they differ in metaphysics, selfhood, virtue, and practice. Both can be useful without flattening them into the same philosophy.

nodes.label: Stoicism — Core Principles
nodes.excerpt: Stoicism teaches that virtue, judgment, and what is within one's control matter more than external fortune. Its core value today is disciplined agency without denial of emotion or reality.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Stoicism — Daily Practices (Negative Visualization, Memento Mori, Amor Fati)
nodes.label: Stoicism — Daily Practices (Negative Visualization, Memento Mori, Amor Fati)
nodes.excerpt: Stoic daily practices train perspective, gratitude, courage, and steadiness. Negative visualization, memento mori, and amor fati are tools for relating better to uncertainty, loss, and difficulty.

nodes.label: Taoism Key Concepts (Wu Wei, Yin-Yang)
nodes.excerpt: Taoism emphasizes living in accordance with the Tao, balancing opposites, simplicity, humility, and effortless action. Wu wei is not laziness; it is skillful action without unnecessary force.

nodes.label: The Simulation Hypothesis
nodes.excerpt: The simulation hypothesis argues that our experienced reality could be generated by a more fundamental computational system. It modernizes skepticism with computing language, but remains difficult to test.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism — Bentham and Mill
nodes.excerpt: Utilitarianism is a consequentialist ethical theory that evaluates actions by overall welfare or happiness. Bentham emphasized quantity of pleasure, while Mill distinguished higher and lower pleasures.

nodes.label: Zen Buddhism Basics
nodes.excerpt: Zen Buddhism emphasizes direct experience, meditation, non-attachment, impermanence, and seeing through conceptual fixation. Its practical value is attention to what is actually present.

nodes.label: Chaos Theory and Complexity
nodes.excerpt: Chaos theory studies deterministic systems that are highly sensitive to initial conditions, while complexity studies systems with many interacting parts and emergent behavior. Both explain why simple rules can produce hard-to-predict outcomes.

nodes.label: Classical Mechanics Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Classical mechanics studies motion, forces, energy, momentum, and rotation at everyday scales. It is the foundation for engineering intuition even though relativity and quantum mechanics supersede it in extreme regimes.

nodes.label: Dark Matter and Dark Energy
nodes.excerpt: Dark matter and dark energy are names for major gaps in cosmology: unseen gravitational mass and the accelerated expansion of the universe. Together they dominate standard cosmological models, but their underlying nature remains unknown.

nodes.label: Electromagnetism Basics (Maxwell's Equations, Intuition)
nodes.excerpt: Electromagnetism unifies electric and magnetic fields, charges, currents, light, and electromagnetic waves. Maxwell's equations describe how fields are created, interact, and propagate.

nodes.label: Entropy — Physical and Information Interpretations
nodes.excerpt: Entropy measures multiplicity, uncertainty, or missing information depending on context. In physics it relates to the number of microstates compatible with a macrostate; in information theory it measures uncertainty in messages.

nodes.label: Fermi Estimation (Orders of Magnitude Thinking)
nodes.excerpt: Fermi estimation uses rough assumptions and orders of magnitude to estimate unknown quantities. It is useful for physics, engineering, product sizing, system design, and sanity-checking claims.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/General Relativity — Key Ideas (Spacetime, Gravity as Curvature)
nodes.label: General Relativity — Key Ideas (Spacetime, Gravity as Curvature)
nodes.excerpt: General relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy. Objects follow paths through curved spacetime, explaining orbits, gravitational time dilation, black holes, and cosmic expansion.

nodes.label: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
nodes.excerpt: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle says certain pairs of properties, such as position and momentum, cannot both be known with arbitrary precision. This is a structural feature of quantum systems, not just measurement clumsiness.

nodes.label: Information Theory (Shannon Entropy)
nodes.excerpt: Information theory quantifies uncertainty, communication, compression, and noise. Shannon entropy measures expected information content and connects communication engineering to probability, physics, and machine learning.

nodes.label: Many-Worlds Interpretation of Quantum Mechanics
nodes.excerpt: The Many-Worlds interpretation says the quantum wavefunction evolves without collapse, and measurement corresponds to branching into non-interacting outcomes. It is an interpretation of quantum mechanics, not a proven multiverse story.

nodes.label: Phase Transitions and Emergence
nodes.excerpt: Phase transitions occur when systems shift qualitatively as conditions change, such as water freezing or magnets ordering. Emergence describes higher-level behavior that arises from many lower-level interactions.

nodes.label: Quantum Computing Basics (Qubits, Superposition, Entanglement)
nodes.excerpt: Quantum computing uses qubits, superposition, entanglement, and interference to perform certain computations differently from classical computers. It is promising for specific problem classes, not a universal faster computer.

nodes.label: Quantum Entanglement and Superposition
nodes.excerpt: Superposition allows quantum systems to exist in combinations of states, while entanglement creates correlations between systems that cannot be explained classically. These ideas are central to quantum information and quantum computing.

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanics Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Quantum mechanics describes physical systems at microscopic scales using states, amplitudes, measurement probabilities, operators, and quantized observables. It replaces classical certainty with probabilistic structure.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/Special Relativity — Key Ideas (Time Dilation, Length Contraction)
nodes.label: Special Relativity — Key Ideas (Time Dilation, Length Contraction)
nodes.excerpt: Special relativity shows that time and space measurements depend on relative motion, while the speed of light is invariant. Time dilation, length contraction, and mass-energy equivalence follow from this structure.

nodes.label: String Theory — Accessible Overview
nodes.excerpt: String theory proposes that fundamental entities are tiny vibrating strings rather than point particles, offering a possible framework for quantum gravity. It is mathematically rich but experimentally unconfirmed.

nodes.label: The Big Bang Theory
nodes.excerpt: The Big Bang theory describes the universe expanding and cooling from an early hot, dense state. Its evidence includes cosmic expansion, the cosmic microwave background, and light-element abundances.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/The Standard Model of Particle Physics
nodes.label: The Standard Model of Particle Physics
nodes.excerpt: The Standard Model describes known elementary particles and three fundamental interactions: electromagnetic, weak, and strong forces. It is extremely successful, but does not include gravity, dark matter, or dark energy.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/Thermodynamics and the Arrow of Time
nodes.label: Thermodynamics and the Arrow of Time
nodes.excerpt: The arrow of time is the observed direction from past to future, closely tied to entropy increase. Many microscopic laws are time-symmetric, but macroscopic systems overwhelmingly move toward higher-entropy states.

nodes.label: Thermodynamics — Four Laws
nodes.excerpt: Thermodynamics studies heat, work, energy, temperature, and entropy in physical systems. Its four laws define thermal equilibrium, energy conservation, entropy increase, and the unattainability of absolute zero.

nodes.label: Wave-Particle Duality
nodes.excerpt: Wave-particle duality is the quantum idea that entities like photons and electrons show wave-like or particle-like behavior depending on experimental context. They are not simply classical waves or classical particles.

nodes.label: Process Reward Models
nodes.excerpt: step-level rewards vs outcome rewards: credit assignment, PRM800K, Monte Carlo estimation, test-time compute scaling via MCTS.

nodes.label: Product Engineering
nodes.excerpt: product engineering guide covering problem framing, MVPs, activation, retention, engagement, funnels, user research, prioritization, onboarding, analytics, and project-specific product risks.

nodes.label: Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: When to use each programming language and why — covering the core mental model (control vs. convenience, performance predictability), plus deep reasoning for every major language from C to COBOL, organized by domain.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/C — Memory Management and Pointers
nodes.label: C — Memory Management and Pointers
nodes.excerpt: C gives direct control over memory through pointers, stack allocation, heap allocation, and manual lifetime management. That control enables systems programming but makes bugs such as use-after-free, buffer overflow, and leaks easy to create.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Compiled vs Interpreted vs JIT Languages
nodes.label: Compiled vs Interpreted vs JIT Languages
nodes.excerpt: Compiled, interpreted, and JIT execution models describe when code is translated and optimized. The boundaries are blurry in modern runtimes, but the model affects startup time, portability, debugging, deployment, and peak performance.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Concurrency Primitives (Mutex, Semaphore, Condition Variable)
nodes.label: Concurrency Primitives (Mutex, Semaphore, Condition Variable)
