nodes.excerpt: Concurrency primitives are low-level coordination tools for sharing resources safely across threads or tasks. Mutexes protect critical sections, semaphores limit access counts, and condition variables let workers sleep until shared state changes.

nodes.label: DSL Design
nodes.excerpt: A domain-specific language is a small language designed around a narrow problem domain. Good DSLs make domain intent clear and prevent invalid states, while bad DSLs become underpowered programming languages with custom syntax and weak tooling.

nodes.label: Functional Programming Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Functional programming emphasizes functions as values, immutability, expression-oriented code, composition, and avoiding hidden side effects. Its ideas appear in modern JavaScript, Python, Rust, Scala, Haskell, Elixir, and many UI/data workflows.

nodes.label: Garbage Collection Algorithms (Mark-Sweep, Generational)
nodes.excerpt: Garbage collection algorithms reclaim memory that a program can no longer reach. Mark-sweep, copying, compacting, reference counting, and generational collectors make different tradeoffs among throughput, pause time, fragmentation, and implementation complexity.

nodes.label: Go — Goroutines and Channels
nodes.excerpt: Go goroutines are lightweight concurrent functions managed by the Go runtime, and channels communicate values between goroutines. They support Go's CSP-inspired concurrency style, but still require careful cancellation, buffering, and ownership design.

nodes.label: Go — Interface System
nodes.excerpt: Go interfaces define behavior implicitly: a type satisfies an interface by implementing its methods. This keeps abstractions lightweight, but interface design should stay small, consumer-owned, and behavior-focused.

nodes.label: Immutability and Pure Functions
nodes.excerpt: Immutability and pure functions are core functional programming ideas that make code easier to reason about, test, parallelize, and refactor. They reduce hidden state changes by treating data as values and making effects explicit.

nodes.label: JIT Compilation
nodes.excerpt: Just-in-time compilation turns code into optimized machine code while a program is running. JITs trade startup and runtime complexity for adaptive optimization based on real execution behavior.

nodes.label: Java — Garbage Collection
nodes.excerpt: Java garbage collection automatically reclaims unreachable objects from the JVM heap. Different collectors optimize for throughput, latency, pause time, footprint, or heap size, so GC behavior is an operational concern.

nodes.label: Java — JVM Internals
nodes.excerpt: The JVM executes platform-independent bytecode with class loading, verification, interpretation, JIT compilation, garbage collection, and runtime services. JVM internals explain Java's portability, performance profile, and operational behavior.

nodes.label: JavaScript — Closures
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript closures let functions retain access to variables from their lexical scope after the outer function has returned. They enable callbacks, encapsulation, function factories, and many async patterns.

nodes.label: JavaScript — Promises and Async-Await
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript promises represent future completion or failure of asynchronous work, and async/await provides structured syntax for using them. Understanding promise states, chaining, microtasks, and error propagation is essential for modern JS.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/JavaScript — Prototypes and Prototype Chain
nodes.label: JavaScript — Prototypes and Prototype Chain
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript inheritance is prototype-based: objects delegate property lookup through a prototype chain. Classes in modern JavaScript are syntax over this prototype model, not a separate class-based runtime.

nodes.label: JavaScript — The Event Loop
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript's event loop coordinates call stack execution, queued tasks, microtasks, timers, and asynchronous callbacks. It explains why nonblocking I/O works and why long synchronous work freezes the page or server.

nodes.label: Language Design Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: Language design is a set of tradeoffs among safety, performance, expressiveness, simplicity, tooling, compatibility, and ecosystem goals. Good language choices are contextual, not universal.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Memory Models and Happens-Before Ordering
nodes.label: Memory Models and Happens-Before Ordering
nodes.excerpt: A language memory model defines what reads and writes are allowed to do in concurrent programs. Happens-before ordering is the reasoning tool that tells whether one thread's writes must be visible to another.

nodes.label: Metaprogramming and Code Generation
nodes.excerpt: Metaprogramming and code generation let programs create, inspect, or transform other programs. They can remove repetition and encode patterns, but they also risk hiding behavior behind machinery that is harder to read and debug.

nodes.label: Monads (Accessible Explanation)
nodes.excerpt: Monads are a functional programming abstraction for chaining computations that carry context, such as optional values, errors, async work, state, or I/O. The practical idea is not mystical: wrap a value, provide a way to chain the next step, and let the wrapper handle the context.

nodes.label: Python — Async-Await and asyncio
nodes.excerpt: Python async/await and asyncio provide cooperative concurrency for many waiting tasks. They are strongest for I/O-heavy workloads where coroutines can yield control while sockets, timers, subprocesses, or queues wait.

nodes.label: Python — Context Managers
nodes.excerpt: Python context managers define setup and cleanup around a block using the `with` statement. They make resource handling deterministic for files, locks, transactions, temporary state, and cleanup-heavy workflows.

nodes.label: Python — Decorators Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Python decorators wrap functions, methods, or classes to modify behavior without changing the decorated object's source body. They are useful for logging, caching, authorization, validation, registration, retries, and framework metadata.

nodes.label: Python — GIL and Concurrency
nodes.excerpt: Python concurrency is shaped by CPython's Global Interpreter Lock, which historically allowed only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. New free-threaded CPython builds change that story, but compatibility and extension support still matter.

nodes.label: Python — Generators and Iterators
nodes.excerpt: Python iterators provide a uniform protocol for pulling values one at a time, while generators create iterators with paused function state. They make lazy pipelines, streaming data, and memory-efficient traversal natural.

nodes.label: Python — Internals and CPython
nodes.excerpt: CPython is the reference Python implementation, built around bytecode execution, Python objects, reference counting, extension modules, and a large C runtime. Understanding CPython internals explains Python's performance, memory behavior, C extension ecosystem, and concurrency tradeoffs.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Python — Memory Management and gc
nodes.label: Python — Memory Management and gc
nodes.excerpt: Python memory management in CPython combines reference counting, object allocation layers, and cyclic garbage collection. The `gc` module exposes controls for the cycle detector that supplements reference counting.

nodes.label: Python — Metaclasses
nodes.excerpt: Python metaclasses customize how classes themselves are created. They are powerful for frameworks, registries, validation, and DSL-like APIs, but are usually unnecessary for ordinary application code.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Python — Type System and mypy
nodes.label: Python — Type System and mypy
nodes.excerpt: Python's type hints add optional static information to a dynamic language, and mypy checks those annotations before runtime. The goal is better maintainability and earlier bug detection without giving up Python's runtime flexibility.

nodes.label: Rust — Lifetimes
nodes.excerpt: Rust lifetimes describe how long references are valid so the compiler can prevent dangling references. Most lifetimes are inferred, but explicit annotations are needed when relationships between borrowed values are ambiguous.

nodes.label: Rust — Ownership and Borrowing
nodes.excerpt: Rust ownership and borrowing enforce memory safety without a garbage collector. Values have owners, references borrow values, and the compiler prevents dangling references and data races through strict rules.

nodes.label: Rust — Traits and Generics
nodes.excerpt: Rust traits define shared behavior, and generics let code work over many concrete types with trait bounds. Together they provide static polymorphism, reusable APIs, and zero-cost abstraction patterns.

nodes.label: TypeScript — Generics
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript generics let functions, classes, and types operate over type parameters while preserving relationships between inputs and outputs. They are the foundation for reusable typed APIs.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/TypeScript — Type System Deep Dive
nodes.label: TypeScript — Type System Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript adds a structural static type system on top of JavaScript. Its type checker models runtime JavaScript patterns with unions, intersections, generics, narrowing, conditional types, and inference.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/TypeScript — Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit, etc.)
nodes.label: TypeScript — Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit, etc.)
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript utility types transform existing types into related shapes, such as optional fields, selected keys, omitted keys, readonly objects, or required properties. They reduce duplication and keep API types synchronized.

nodes.label: WebAssembly (WASM) Basics
nodes.excerpt: WebAssembly is a portable low-level bytecode format designed for safe, near-native execution in browsers and other runtimes. It lets languages like Rust, C, and C++ run in web contexts while preserving sandboxing and predictable performance.

nodes.label: RAG and Bobby Vector Search
nodes.excerpt: what RAG is, how Bobby's current index-based retrieval works, and when to upgrade to vector search (~300 notes trigger).

nodes.label: RLHF and Alignment
nodes.excerpt: aligning LLMs: SFT, reward modeling, PPO, DPO (with math), Constitutional AI, steering vectors, reward hacking cases, alignment tax.

nodes.label: Rate Limiting
nodes.excerpt: five algorithms (token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window log, sliding window counter), distributed enforcement with Redis, where to enforce, rate limit headers.

nodes.label: VanossGaming - Dewit
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Tyler says \"Dewit\" at 0:53, meaning \"Do it.\".

nodes.id: Resources/References/VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.label: VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Nogla slurs \"bow and arrow\" into \"boner\" at 3:13 while Evan trolls him invisibly.

nodes.label: Scaling
nodes.excerpt: vertical vs. horizontal scaling, sharding strategies (range/hash/consistent hashing/directory), cross-shard queries, replication, and tail latency mitigation (hedged requests, LIFO queues, load shedding).

nodes.label: Search Systems
nodes.excerpt: inverted index, text processing pipeline, TF-IDF vs BM25, Elasticsearch internals (shards, segments, near-real-time write path), autocomplete (trie, edge n-grams, fuzzy), vector search (HNSW), hybrid search with RRF.

nodes.label: Security
nodes.excerpt: threat modeling, authn vs authz, session auth, JWT (alg:none attack, refresh rotation), bcrypt/Argon2, OWASP Top 10, XSS/CSRF/SSRF/race conditions, secrets management, security headers.

nodes.label: API Key Management
nodes.excerpt: API key management controls how keys are created, scoped, stored, rotated, monitored, and revoked. Good key design limits blast radius with least privilege, environment separation, usage limits, and leak detection.

nodes.label: Authentication vs Authorization
nodes.excerpt: Authentication verifies who a user or service is, while authorization decides what that identity can do. Confusing the two leads to systems that know the user but still expose the wrong data or actions.

nodes.label: CSRF — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks a browser into sending authenticated state-changing requests from another site. Prevention uses SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, origin checks, and avoiding unsafe state changes on GET.

nodes.label: Clickjacking Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Clickjacking tricks users into interacting with a hidden or disguised page embedded in another site. Prevention uses frame restrictions such as CSP frame-ancestors and X-Frame-Options, plus UI design for sensitive actions.

nodes.label: Container Security
nodes.excerpt: Container security protects images, registries, runtimes, orchestrators, secrets, and host boundaries. Good practice includes minimal images, vulnerability scanning, non-root users, least privileges, signed artifacts, and controlled runtime permissions.

nodes.label: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Cross-site scripting happens when attacker-controlled content runs as JavaScript in another user's browser. Prevention relies on contextual output encoding, sanitization for allowed HTML, safe frameworks, CSP, and avoiding dangerous DOM APIs.

nodes.label: Data Privacy Regulations (GDPR, CCPA)
nodes.excerpt: GDPR and CCPA are major privacy regulations that shape how organizations collect, process, disclose, delete, and protect personal data. Engineering impact shows up in consent, data inventory, deletion, access requests, retention, and vendor controls.

nodes.label: Defense in Depth
nodes.excerpt: Defense in depth layers independent controls so one failure does not become total compromise. It combines preventive, detective, and responsive controls across code, identity, network, data, infrastructure, and operations.

nodes.label: Encryption — Symmetric vs Asymmetric
nodes.excerpt: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption, while asymmetric encryption uses public/private key pairs. Real systems often combine them: asymmetric cryptography establishes trust and symmetric keys protect bulk data.

nodes.label: End-to-End Encryption
nodes.excerpt: End-to-end encryption protects message contents so only the communicating endpoints can read them. Servers may route, store, and synchronize ciphertext, but they should not possess the keys needed to decrypt user content.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/JWT — Structure, Signing, and Security
nodes.label: JWT — Structure, Signing, and Security
nodes.excerpt: JWTs are signed JSON tokens made of header, payload, and signature sections. They are useful for portable claims, but security depends on validation, key management, expiration, audience checks, and avoiding sensitive payload data.

nodes.label: OAuth 2.0 Flow
nodes.excerpt: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework for delegated access, letting clients access resources with tokens instead of user passwords. The most common secure web flow is Authorization Code with PKCE.

nodes.label: OWASP Top 10
nodes.excerpt: The OWASP Top 10 is a widely used awareness document for the most critical web application security risks, with OWASP listing the 2025 release as the current version. It is a starting point for secure engineering, not a complete security program.

nodes.label: OpenID Connect (OIDC)
nodes.excerpt: OpenID Connect adds an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 so applications can authenticate users. It introduces ID tokens, standard user claims, discovery metadata, and login-oriented flows.

nodes.label: PII Handling Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: PII handling best practices reduce risk around data that identifies or can be linked to people. The core habits are minimization, classification, access control, encryption, retention limits, auditability, and careful logging.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/Passkeys and Hardware Security Keys (WebAuthn)
nodes.label: Passkeys and Hardware Security Keys (WebAuthn)
nodes.excerpt: Passkeys and hardware security keys use public-key cryptography through WebAuthn to provide phishing-resistant authentication. They reduce reliance on shared secrets by proving possession of a private key bound to the real site origin.

nodes.label: Password Hashing (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt)
nodes.excerpt: Password hashing protects stored passwords by using slow, salted, one-way functions such as bcrypt, Argon2, or scrypt. The goal is to make offline cracking expensive even if the password database leaks.

nodes.label: Penetration Testing Overview
nodes.excerpt: Penetration testing is an authorized attempt to find exploitable security weaknesses in systems, applications, networks, or processes. Its value depends on scope, rules of engagement, realistic attack paths, evidence, and actionable remediation.

nodes.label: Phishing — Recognition and Defense
nodes.excerpt: Phishing tricks users into revealing credentials, installing malware, approving access, or taking unsafe actions. Defense requires user reporting, email security, MFA, domain protections, safe links, and resilient account recovery.

nodes.label: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
nodes.excerpt: Public Key Infrastructure manages certificates, certificate authorities, trust chains, issuance, renewal, and revocation. PKI is how browsers, services, and organizations bind public keys to identities at scale.

nodes.label: SQL Injection — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: SQL injection occurs when untrusted input becomes executable SQL. Prevention depends on parameterized queries, safe query builders, least-privilege database accounts, validation, and avoiding string-built SQL.

nodes.label: Secrets Scanning (GitGuardian, truffleHog)
nodes.excerpt: Secrets scanning detects leaked credentials such as API keys, tokens, private keys, and passwords in code, commits, logs, and artifacts. Tools like GitGuardian and truffleHog reduce exposure, but leaked secrets still require rotation and incident response.

nodes.label: Security Auditing
nodes.excerpt: Security auditing evaluates whether systems, processes, access, and evidence match expected security controls. Good audits are scoped, evidence-based, risk-oriented, and followed by concrete remediation.

nodes.label: Security Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
nodes.excerpt: Security headers tell browsers to enforce protections around scripts, transport security, framing, content sniffing, referrers, and permissions. They are low-cost defense-in-depth controls, but they must be configured deliberately and tested.

nodes.label: Session Management
nodes.excerpt: Session management keeps users authenticated across requests while controlling token lifetime, storage, renewal, invalidation, and theft risk. Secure sessions depend on cookie flags, rotation, expiration, CSRF defenses, and server-side authorization checks.

nodes.label: Social Engineering Attacks
nodes.excerpt: Social engineering attacks manipulate people rather than directly exploiting software. They work by abusing trust, urgency, authority, fear, helpfulness, or routine operational habits.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Security
nodes.excerpt: Supply chain security protects the software, dependencies, build systems, CI/CD pipelines, artifacts, vendors, and update channels a system relies on. It matters because attackers can compromise trusted inputs instead of attacking production directly.

nodes.label: Threat Modeling (STRIDE)
nodes.excerpt: Threat modeling identifies what can go wrong in a system before attackers or incidents expose it. STRIDE is a practical framework covering spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial of service, and elevation of privilege.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/Vulnerability Disclosure and Bug Bounty Programs
nodes.label: Vulnerability Disclosure and Bug Bounty Programs
nodes.excerpt: Vulnerability disclosure and bug bounty programs define how external researchers report security issues and how organizations triage, fix, reward, and communicate them. Clear scope and response expectations prevent chaos.

nodes.label: Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
nodes.excerpt: Zero-day vulnerabilities are security flaws unknown to the vendor or unpatched in affected systems. Defending against them depends on reducing blast radius, monitoring behavior, patch readiness, and layered controls.

nodes.label: Zero-Trust Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Zero-trust architecture assumes network location alone should not grant trust. Every request should be authenticated, authorized, encrypted, monitored, and limited according to identity, device, context, and policy.

nodes.label: Security Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper security guide covering OAuth/OIDC, sessions vs JWTs, passkeys, XSS/CSRF/SSRF, SQL injection, supply chain, container/Kubernetes security, secrets, threat modeling, and incident response.

nodes.label: Alcohol and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol can make sleep onset feel easier but usually worsens sleep quality by fragmenting sleep, reducing REM early in the night, increasing awakenings, and worsening breathing issues. It is a poor sleep aid.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Blue Light and Melatonin Suppression
nodes.label: Blue Light and Melatonin Suppression
nodes.excerpt: Blue-enriched evening light can suppress melatonin and delay circadian timing, especially when bright and close to bedtime. Light timing, brightness, duration, and personal sensitivity all matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Chronotypes (Early Bird vs Night Owl — Genetics)
nodes.label: Chronotypes (Early Bird vs Night Owl — Genetics)
nodes.excerpt: Chronotype is a person's natural tendency toward earlier or later sleep-wake timing, influenced by genetics, age, light exposure, and schedule. It is real, but habits and environment still shape actual sleep timing.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Circadian Rhythm — How It Works
nodes.label: Circadian Rhythm — How It Works
nodes.excerpt: Circadian rhythm is the roughly 24-hour body clock shaped by light, timing, meals, activity, and routine. Morning light and consistent wake time are strong anchors for sleep timing.

nodes.label: Jet Lag Management
nodes.excerpt: Jet lag happens when the internal circadian clock is misaligned with the destination schedule. Light timing, meal timing, sleep scheduling, caffeine, and sometimes melatonin can help shift rhythm.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Melatonin Supplementation — When and How Much
nodes.label: Melatonin Supplementation — When and How Much
nodes.excerpt: Melatonin is a circadian timing signal, not a general knockout sleep drug. It may help jet lag or delayed sleep timing, but product quality, timing, dose, medication interactions, and unknown long-term safety all matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Napping — Types and Optimal Duration (Power Nap, Full Cycle)
nodes.label: Napping — Types and Optimal Duration (Power Nap, Full Cycle)
nodes.excerpt: Naps can reduce sleep pressure and improve alertness, but timing and duration determine whether they help or create grogginess. Short power naps are often safer for daytime alertness than long late naps.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Noise and Sleep (White, Pink, Brown Noise)
nodes.label: Noise and Sleep (White, Pink, Brown Noise)
nodes.excerpt: Noise can fragment sleep even when it does not fully wake someone. White, pink, or brown noise may help by masking unpredictable sounds, but volume and personal comfort matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Pre-Sleep Nutrition and Sleep Quality
nodes.label: Pre-Sleep Nutrition and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Pre-sleep nutrition can affect sleep through digestion, blood sugar, reflux, caffeine, alcohol, hydration, and meal size. The best pattern is individual, but heavy late meals and alcohol often hurt sleep quality.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Cycles and Ultradian Rhythms
nodes.label: Sleep Cycles and Ultradian Rhythms
nodes.excerpt: Sleep cycles repeat roughly every 90 minutes, moving through NREM and REM stages. Early sleep tends to contain more deep sleep, while later sleep tends to contain more REM.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Deprivation — Cognitive and Physical Effects
nodes.label: Sleep Deprivation — Cognitive and Physical Effects
nodes.excerpt: Sleep deprivation impairs attention, reaction time, learning, mood, immune function, metabolic health, and physical performance. Chronic sleep restriction can be especially deceptive because people adapt subjectively while performance remains impaired.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Disorders Overview (Apnea, Insomnia, RLS)
nodes.label: Sleep Disorders Overview (Apnea, Insomnia, RLS)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep disorders like sleep apnea, insomnia, and restless legs syndrome can impair health and daytime function beyond ordinary poor habits. Persistent symptoms, loud snoring, choking awakenings, or severe daytime sleepiness deserve medical evaluation.

nodes.label: Sleep Hygiene Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Sleep hygiene is the set of habits and environmental choices that support reliable sleep: consistent wake time, morning light, reduced evening stimulation, caffeine timing, cool room, and a repeatable wind-down.

nodes.label: Sleep Pressure and Adenosine
nodes.excerpt: Sleep pressure builds during wakefulness partly through adenosine accumulation and decreases during sleep. Caffeine masks this pressure by blocking adenosine receptors rather than removing the underlying need for sleep.

nodes.label: Sleep Stages (NREM 1-2-3 and REM)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep cycles through NREM and REM stages, each associated with different brain and body functions. Deep NREM supports physical restoration and memory consolidation, while REM is linked to dreaming, emotion, and learning.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Tracking — Metrics That Actually Matter
nodes.label: Sleep Tracking — Metrics That Actually Matter
nodes.excerpt: Sleep tracking is useful for trends like duration, consistency, awakenings, resting heart rate, HRV, and subjective recovery. Consumer sleep-stage estimates are less reliable and should not drive anxiety.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Social Jetlag — The Weekend Sleep Shift
nodes.label: Social Jetlag — The Weekend Sleep Shift
nodes.excerpt: Social jetlag is the mismatch between biological sleep timing and social schedules, often seen when weekday and weekend sleep times shift. Large shifts can make Monday feel like crossing time zones.

nodes.label: Temperature and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Sleep quality is affected by thermoregulation, and many people sleep better in a cool room with breathable bedding. Temperature needs are individual, but overheating commonly fragments sleep.

nodes.label: Software Architecture
nodes.excerpt: architecture guide covering layered, hexagonal, clean architecture, DDD, modular monoliths, microservices, event-driven design, CQRS, dependency inversion, and abstraction tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Software Engineering Tools
nodes.excerpt: full survey of tools modern engineering teams use: design (Figma), IDEs (VS Code, Cursor, Zed), AI assistants (Copilot, Claude Code), CI/CD, observability, databases, and communication tools.

nodes.label: 12-Factor App Methodology
nodes.excerpt: The 12-factor app methodology is a set of practices for building portable, deployable, cloud-friendly services. It emphasizes declarative config, stateless processes, clean dependencies, logs as streams, and strict separation between build and run.

nodes.label: API Design — GraphQL
nodes.excerpt: GraphQL is an API query language and runtime that lets clients request exactly the data shape they need. It is strongest for product surfaces that aggregate many backend entities, but it requires discipline around schema design, authorization, and query cost control.

nodes.label: API Design — REST Principles
nodes.excerpt: REST API design uses HTTP resources, methods, status codes, and representations to expose predictable service interfaces. Good REST design is less about purity and more about stable contracts, clear semantics, and operational usability.

nodes.label: API Design — Server-Sent Events
nodes.excerpt: Server-Sent Events are a simple HTTP-based way for servers to stream updates to clients. SSE is a good fit for one-way live updates where WebSockets would add unnecessary connection and protocol complexity.

nodes.label: API Design — WebSockets
nodes.excerpt: WebSockets provide a persistent bidirectional connection between client and server. They are useful for low-latency interactive features, but they require explicit handling for connection state, scaling, authentication, and backpressure.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.label: API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.excerpt: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework that uses Protocol Buffers to define typed service contracts. It is commonly used for internal service communication where latency, streaming, and strongly typed generated clients matter.

nodes.label: Anti-Patterns and Code Smells
nodes.excerpt: Anti-patterns and code smells are warning signs that a design may be harder to understand, test, or change than it needs to be. They are not automatic proof of bad code, but they are prompts for closer inspection.

nodes.label: Backward Compatibility Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Backward compatibility lets existing clients continue working as systems evolve. It requires additive changes, deprecation windows, schema evolution discipline, and careful deployment ordering.

nodes.label: Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)
nodes.excerpt: Behavior-Driven Development extends test-first development by describing system behavior in language shared by engineering, product, and domain stakeholders. It is most useful when examples clarify requirements across roles.

nodes.label: Blue-Green Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Blue-green deployment keeps two production environments so traffic can switch quickly between old and new versions. It reduces release risk when environments are truly equivalent and rollback is safe.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.label: Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.excerpt: Build systems turn source code into tested, packaged, runnable artifacts. Tools like Make, Bazel, and Nx differ in how much structure they provide for dependency graphs, caching, reproducibility, and monorepo scale.

nodes.label: CQRS Pattern
nodes.excerpt: CQRS separates commands that change state from queries that read state. It is useful when write models and read models have different complexity, performance, or scaling needs.

nodes.label: Canary Releases
nodes.excerpt: Canary releases expose a new version to a small subset of traffic before full rollout. They reduce blast radius and use real production signals to decide whether to proceed.

nodes.label: Chaos Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Chaos engineering deliberately injects controlled failures to test whether systems remain resilient. It turns reliability assumptions into experiments before real incidents expose them.

nodes.label: Clean Code Principles
nodes.excerpt: Clean code principles explain how to write software that other engineers can safely read, change, and extend. The practical focus is clarity, intention-revealing structure, and reducing surprise rather than chasing aesthetic purity.

nodes.label: Code Complexity — Cyclomatic Complexity
nodes.excerpt: Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of independent paths through code. It is a useful warning signal for testing and maintainability, but it should be interpreted with context rather than treated as a universal score.

nodes.label: Code Review Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Code review is a collaborative quality gate for correctness, maintainability, security, and shared understanding. Strong reviews focus on risk and clarity rather than personal preference.

nodes.label: Container Networking
nodes.excerpt: Container networking connects isolated container processes to hosts, other containers, services, and external networks. Understanding bridges, overlays, ports, DNS, and network policies is essential for debugging containerized systems.

nodes.label: Conventional Commits
nodes.excerpt: Conventional Commits is a commit-message convention that adds machine-readable intent to project history. It supports changelog generation, semantic versioning, and easier review of change purpose.

nodes.label: Dependency Management
nodes.excerpt: Dependency management controls how projects select, version, update, audit, and isolate third-party or internal packages. It affects security, reliability, reproducibility, and long-term maintenance.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.excerpt: Behavioral design patterns organize runtime interaction between objects. They help separate algorithms, events, state transitions, commands, and traversal logic from the objects that use them.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.excerpt: Creational design patterns centralize object construction so callers do not need to know every setup detail. They are most valuable when construction has branching logic, required sequencing, or expensive shared resources.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.excerpt: Structural design patterns organize how objects and interfaces fit together. They help engineers adapt incompatible APIs, compose nested structures, add behavior safely, and hide subsystem complexity.

nodes.label: Docker Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Docker packages applications into images and runs them as isolated containers. Its practical value is consistent development, CI, and deployment environments, but production use requires attention to layers, networking, volumes, and security.

nodes.label: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
nodes.excerpt: Domain-Driven Design is an approach to modeling software around the language, rules, and boundaries of the business domain. Its most useful ideas are ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, and keeping domain logic explicit.

nodes.label: Event Sourcing
nodes.excerpt: Event sourcing stores every state-changing event as the source of truth instead of only storing the latest state. It enables auditability and temporal reconstruction, but requires careful event schema, replay, and operational discipline.

nodes.label: Event-Driven Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Event-driven architecture coordinates systems through published facts about things that happened. It decouples producers from consumers, but shifts complexity into event design, ordering, idempotency, and observability.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.label: Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.excerpt: Feature flags decouple deploying code from releasing behavior. Progressive rollout uses flags to expose changes gradually, measure impact, and quickly disable risky behavior.

nodes.label: Git Flow vs Trunk-Based
nodes.excerpt: Git Flow and trunk-based development are contrasting branching models. Git Flow emphasizes release branches and staged stabilization, while trunk-based development emphasizes frequent integration into main.

nodes.label: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
nodes.excerpt: Hexagonal architecture places domain logic at the center and connects external systems through ports and adapters. It helps keep business rules independent from frameworks, databases, and transport details.

nodes.label: Integration Testing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Integration testing verifies that multiple components work together across real boundaries such as databases, APIs, queues, and filesystems. It catches contract and configuration bugs that unit tests intentionally ignore.

nodes.label: Kubernetes Basics
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes is a container orchestration system that keeps declared application state running across a cluster. It manages scheduling, service discovery, rollouts, health checks, scaling, and recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.label: Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.excerpt: Load and performance testing measure how a system behaves under expected, peak, and stressful traffic. The goal is to find capacity limits, latency bottlenecks, and failure modes before users do.

nodes.label: Microservices Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Microservices architecture decomposes a system into independently deployable services aligned around business capabilities. It can improve team autonomy and scaling, but it introduces distributed systems complexity that must be justified.

nodes.label: Monolith vs Microservices Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: The monolith versus microservices decision is a tradeoff between simplicity and independent service autonomy. The right answer depends on team size, domain boundaries, deployment needs, and operational maturity.

nodes.label: Monorepo vs Polyrepo
nodes.excerpt: Monorepo and polyrepo strategies organize source code around different tradeoffs in coordination, tooling, ownership, and release independence. The best choice depends on team scale, dependency coupling, and build infrastructure maturity.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.label: Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.excerpt: Open source licenses define what others may do with code and what obligations they inherit. Engineers need licensing literacy because dependency choices can affect distribution, compliance, and business risk.

nodes.label: OpenAPI and API Documentation
nodes.excerpt: OpenAPI describes REST-like HTTP APIs in a machine-readable contract. Good API documentation combines accurate schemas with human guidance, examples, error behavior, authentication, and compatibility notes.

nodes.label: Property-Based Testing
nodes.excerpt: Property-based testing checks general invariants over many generated inputs instead of only testing hand-picked examples. It is strongest for parsers, serializers, state machines, algorithms, and code with clear mathematical or domain properties.

nodes.label: Refactoring Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Refactoring is the disciplined process of improving code structure without changing observable behavior. Good refactoring is incremental, test-backed, and focused on reducing the next change's cost.

nodes.label: SOLID Principles
nodes.excerpt: SOLID is a set of object-oriented design principles for keeping classes and modules focused, replaceable, and easier to change. It is useful as a review lens, but harmful when applied mechanically without regard for simpler designs.

nodes.label: Semantic Versioning
nodes.excerpt: Semantic Versioning is a version numbering convention that communicates compatibility expectations through MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH numbers. It helps package users understand whether an update should be safe, additive, or breaking.

nodes.label: Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)
nodes.excerpt: A service mesh adds a dedicated infrastructure layer for service-to-service traffic management, observability, security, and policy. It is powerful in large microservice environments but can be unnecessary operational complexity for simpler systems.

nodes.label: Software Estimation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Software estimation is the practice of forecasting effort, uncertainty, and delivery risk. Good estimates communicate assumptions and ranges, not false precision.

nodes.label: Static Analysis Tools
nodes.excerpt: Static analysis tools inspect code without running it to find bugs, style issues, security risks, type errors, and maintainability problems. They provide cheap automated feedback when configured to match team priorities.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.label: Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.excerpt: Technical debt is the future cost created by implementation choices that make later change harder. Managing it means distinguishing deliberate tradeoffs from accidental decay and paying down the debt that blocks important work.

nodes.label: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
nodes.excerpt: Test-Driven Development is a workflow where tests are written before implementation to clarify desired behavior and guide design. Its value is fastest feedback and design pressure, not simply having more tests.

nodes.label: The Unix Philosophy
nodes.excerpt: The Unix philosophy favors small composable programs that do one thing well and communicate through simple interfaces. Its lasting lesson is that good software tools become powerful when they compose cleanly.

nodes.label: Trunk-Based Development
nodes.excerpt: Trunk-based development is a source-control workflow where engineers integrate small changes into the main branch frequently. It supports continuous integration by reducing long-lived branch drift.

nodes.label: Virtualization — VMs vs Containers
nodes.excerpt: Virtual machines and containers isolate workloads at different layers. VMs virtualize hardware with separate guest operating systems, while containers isolate processes that share the host kernel.

nodes.label: System Design Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: end-to-end design studies for feeds, chat, file sync, collaborative docs, dispatch, payments, video platforms, and job recommendations.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Rubric
nodes.excerpt: 8-phase rubric for FAANG-level system design interviews: requirements, scale estimation, API design, data model, architecture, deep dive, cross-cutting concerns, and evolution.

nodes.label: System Design Notes
nodes.excerpt: scalability, caching, SQL vs NoSQL, queues, load balancing, CAP theorem, rate limiting.

nodes.label: Bulkhead Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The bulkhead pattern isolates resources so one failing dependency or workload cannot exhaust the entire system. It is inspired by ship compartments that prevent one leak from sinking everything.

nodes.label: CAP Theorem
nodes.excerpt: CAP theorem says that during a network partition, a distributed data system must choose between consistency and availability. It is a partition-time tradeoff, not a general excuse for simplistic database labels.

nodes.label: CDN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: A content delivery network caches and serves content from edge locations close to users. CDNs improve latency, absorb traffic spikes, reduce origin load, and add security controls.

nodes.label: Cache Invalidation Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Cache invalidation is the process of removing or updating stale cached data. The right pattern depends on freshness requirements, write frequency, ownership, and tolerance for temporary inconsistency.

nodes.label: Caching Strategies (LRU, LFU, TTL)
nodes.excerpt: Caching stores frequently used data closer to callers to reduce latency, cost, and backend load. LRU, LFU, and TTL are common policies for deciding what stays fresh and what gets evicted.

nodes.label: Circuit Breaker Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The circuit breaker pattern stops calls to a failing dependency before failures cascade. It moves between closed, open, and half-open states based on recent success or failure.

nodes.label: Consensus Algorithms (Raft, Paxos)
nodes.excerpt: Consensus algorithms let distributed nodes agree on a value or ordered log despite failures. Raft and Paxos are foundational protocols for replicated state machines and strongly consistent coordination.

nodes.label: Consistent Hashing
nodes.excerpt: Consistent hashing distributes keys across changing sets of nodes while minimizing remapping. It is useful for caches, sharded storage, load distribution, and systems where nodes frequently join or leave.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.label: Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.excerpt: Content delivery and edge computing move data and computation closer to users. They reduce latency, origin load, and bandwidth costs while introducing cache, consistency, deployment, and observability tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Replication
nodes.excerpt: Database replication copies data from one database node to others for read scaling, high availability, disaster recovery, or geographic locality. Replication design determines consistency, failover behavior, and data-loss risk.

nodes.label: Database Sharding
nodes.excerpt: Database sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple nodes. It increases capacity but introduces routing, resharding, hotspot, transaction, and query-complexity tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Design Twitter X Feed
nodes.excerpt: Designing a Twitter/X feed tests fanout, timeline generation, ranking, social graph storage, media handling, caching, and realtime updates. The core tradeoff is fanout-on-write versus fanout-on-read.

nodes.label: Design Uber Ride-Sharing Backend
nodes.excerpt: Designing an Uber-like backend tests geospatial indexing, realtime location updates, matching, dispatch, pricing, trip state, payments, and reliability. It is a stateful realtime marketplace problem.

nodes.label: Design a Chat Application
nodes.excerpt: Designing a chat application tests realtime delivery, message storage, ordering, presence, fanout, read receipts, offline sync, and notification behavior. It combines realtime systems with durable data modeling.

nodes.label: Design a Distributed Cache
nodes.excerpt: Designing a distributed cache tests key partitioning, replication, eviction, consistency, failure recovery, hot keys, and client routing. It is a core infrastructure system for reducing backend load.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.label: Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.excerpt: Designing an S3-like file storage system tests object APIs, metadata, chunking, durability, replication, consistency, access control, and lifecycle management. The main challenge is cheap durable storage at massive scale.

nodes.label: Design a Notification System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a notification system tests event ingestion, user preferences, aggregation, channel delivery, idempotency, retries, rate limits, and fatigue controls. It separates social/product notifications from transactional reliability needs.

nodes.label: Design a Payment System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a payment system tests correctness, idempotency, ledger modeling, provider integration, retries, reconciliation, fraud checks, and auditability. Reliability matters more than raw throughput.

nodes.label: Design a Rate Limiter
nodes.excerpt: Designing a rate limiter tests algorithms, distributed counters, identity keys, storage, latency, consistency, and failure behavior. The goal is to protect systems while being predictable for legitimate clients.

nodes.label: Design a Recommendation System
nodes.excerpt: Designing a recommendation system tests candidate generation, ranking, feedback loops, feature pipelines, online serving, evaluation, and exploration. The architecture connects ML with product metrics.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.label: Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.excerpt: Designing a Calendly-like scheduling system tests availability calculation, calendar integrations, time zones, booking holds, conflict prevention, notifications, and consistency. Time handling is the hidden complexity.

nodes.label: Design a Search Engine
nodes.excerpt: Designing a search engine tests crawling or ingestion, indexing, ranking, query processing, freshness, autocomplete, and relevance evaluation. Search systems combine data pipelines with low-latency serving.

nodes.label: Design a URL Shortener
nodes.excerpt: Designing a URL shortener tests API design, key generation, redirects, storage, caching, analytics, abuse prevention, and high-read scalability. It is a classic compact system design problem.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.label: Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.excerpt: Designing a video streaming platform tests upload, transcoding, storage, CDN delivery, adaptive bitrate streaming, metadata, recommendations, and analytics. It is a media-heavy system with extreme bandwidth concerns.

nodes.label: Design an API Gateway
nodes.excerpt: Designing an API gateway tests request routing, authentication, authorization, rate limiting, load balancing, observability, protocol translation, and failure handling. It is the front door for many service architectures.

nodes.label: Distributed Locking
nodes.excerpt: Distributed locking coordinates exclusive access to shared resources across multiple processes or machines. It is useful but risky because lock correctness depends on timeouts, ownership, failures, and the underlying consistency model.

nodes.label: Distributed Transactions (2PC, Saga Pattern)
nodes.excerpt: Distributed transactions coordinate changes across multiple services or databases. Two-phase commit preserves atomicity through coordination, while sagas use local transactions and compensating actions.

nodes.label: Eventual Consistency
nodes.excerpt: Eventual consistency means replicas may temporarily disagree but will converge if no new updates occur. It improves availability and latency at the cost of stale reads and conflict handling.

nodes.label: Idempotency in Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: Idempotency means an operation can be applied multiple times with the same intended effect as applying it once. It is essential for safe retries, duplicate messages, and unreliable networks.

nodes.label: Leader Election
nodes.excerpt: Leader election is the process of selecting one node to coordinate work in a distributed system. It is used for primary replicas, schedulers, singleton jobs, and consensus groups.

nodes.label: Load Balancing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple servers to improve capacity, availability, and latency. Strategy choices depend on protocol, health checks, statefulness, geography, and failure behavior.

nodes.label: Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS)
nodes.excerpt: Message queues decouple producers and consumers by storing work or events between them. Kafka, RabbitMQ, and SQS represent different tradeoffs in ordering, durability, throughput, routing, and operations.

nodes.label: PACELC Theorem
nodes.excerpt: PACELC extends CAP by saying distributed systems trade consistency for availability during partitions, else trade latency for consistency during normal operation. It is often more useful than CAP for real architecture decisions.

nodes.label: Pub Sub Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Publish/subscribe architecture lets producers publish messages to topics without knowing which consumers receive them. It is useful for fanout, event-driven integration, and decoupled workflows.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.label: Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.excerpt: Rate limiting controls how many requests a user, client, or system can make in a period. Token bucket, leaky bucket, and sliding-window algorithms trade burst tolerance, smoothness, precision, and storage cost.

nodes.label: Real-Time Systems Design
nodes.excerpt: Real-time systems deliver updates with tight latency expectations. Design choices depend on latency budget, fanout, ordering, delivery guarantees, transport, backpressure, and failure handling.

nodes.label: Scalability — Horizontal vs Vertical
nodes.excerpt: Scalability is a system's ability to handle more load by adding resources or improving efficiency. Vertical scaling makes individual machines stronger, while horizontal scaling adds more machines.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Framework
nodes.excerpt: A system design interview framework gives a repeatable structure for turning vague product prompts into scalable architectures. It keeps candidates from jumping to boxes before clarifying requirements, scale, data, APIs, tradeoffs, and failure modes.

nodes.label: WebRTC Architecture
nodes.excerpt: WebRTC enables low-latency peer-to-peer audio, video, and data communication in browsers and apps. Its architecture combines signaling, ICE, STUN, TURN, RTP media, and optional SFU/MCU servers.

nodes.label: Testing Strategy
nodes.excerpt: testing strategy by layer and risk: unit, integration, E2E, contract, property, load, frontend/backend/database/LLM tests, mocks, flaky tests, and CI.

nodes.label: Transformer Architecture
nodes.excerpt: full transformer internals: tokenization, attention mechanism (Q/K/V), multi-head attention, residual stream, FFN, KV cache, modern improvements (RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU).

nodes.label: Vault Structure
nodes.excerpt: what each folder is for (Projects vs Areas vs Resources vs Maps vs Templates) and the PARA logic behind the structure.

nodes.label: WSL2 Setup
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 gotchas solved: PulseAudio audio fix, Windows host IP, Obsidian portproxy.

nodes.label: Web Animation
nodes.excerpt: CSS transitions/keyframes, GPU-only rule, GSAP, Framer Motion, React Spring, Lottie pipeline, Rive state machines, Three.js/R3F, what makes animations feel good (easing, stagger, spring physics, reduced motion).

nodes.label: Accessibility (a11y) Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Web accessibility means building interfaces that people can use across disabilities, devices, input methods, and assistive technologies. Good accessibility starts with semantic HTML, keyboard support, visible focus, readable contrast, labels, and predictable behavior.

nodes.label: Animation Easing Curves (Cubic Bezier)
nodes.excerpt: Easing curves control how animation progresses over time. Cubic Bezier curves shape acceleration and deceleration, making motion feel responsive, calm, playful, sharp, or heavy.

nodes.label: Browser Security Model (Same-Origin Policy)
nodes.excerpt: The browser security model isolates sites by origin so one site cannot freely read another site's data. Same-origin policy, sandboxing, cookies, CORS, CSP, and storage partitioning are core pieces of web application security.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Browser Storage (localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cookies)
nodes.label: Browser Storage (localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cookies)
nodes.excerpt: Browser storage options differ by capacity, lifetime, sync behavior, security properties, and server visibility. Cookies are for HTTP state, localStorage and sessionStorage are simple synchronous key-value stores, and IndexedDB is the serious client-side database.

nodes.label: CORS — How It Works
nodes.excerpt: CORS is the browser security mechanism that lets servers opt into cross-origin requests. It does not protect servers by itself; it controls which browser-based callers can read responses under the same-origin policy.

nodes.label: CSS Animation and Transitions
nodes.excerpt: CSS transitions and animations are native browser tools for motion. They are strongest for state changes, microinteractions, loaders, and simple choreography, especially when limited to compositor-friendly properties like transform and opacity.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/CSS Architecture (BEM, CSS Modules, Tailwind)
nodes.label: CSS Architecture (BEM, CSS Modules, Tailwind)
nodes.excerpt: CSS architecture is about keeping styling predictable as a codebase grows. BEM, CSS Modules, and Tailwind solve naming, scoping, reuse, and consistency problems in different ways.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/CSS Grid vs Flexbox — When to Use Each
nodes.label: CSS Grid vs Flexbox — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: CSS Grid and Flexbox are complementary layout systems. Flexbox is best for one-dimensional flows, while Grid is best for two-dimensional page and component layouts with explicit rows and columns.

nodes.label: Canvas API
nodes.excerpt: The Canvas API provides immediate-mode drawing for 2D graphics in the browser. It is useful for charts, games, image editing, sketches, and custom visualizations where DOM elements would be too heavy or awkward.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS)
nodes.label: Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS)
nodes.excerpt: Core Web Vitals are user-centered web performance metrics: LCP measures loading, INP measures responsiveness, and CLS measures visual stability. They are useful because they force performance work to focus on what users actually feel.

nodes.label: Critical Rendering Path
nodes.excerpt: The critical rendering path is the sequence of work required before a browser can show the first useful pixels. Optimizing it means reducing render-blocking resources, shortening dependency chains, and prioritizing above-the-fold content.

nodes.label: Design Tokens
nodes.excerpt: Design tokens are named values for visual decisions such as color, spacing, typography, radii, shadows, motion, and breakpoints. They let design systems keep interfaces consistent across components, platforms, and themes.

nodes.label: Framer Motion Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Framer Motion provides declarative React animation patterns for variants, layout animation, gestures, shared layout transitions, and exit animations. It is strongest when motion is tied to React state and component lifecycle.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/How Browsers Work (Parsing, Rendering, Compositing)
nodes.label: How Browsers Work (Parsing, Rendering, Compositing)
nodes.excerpt: Browsers turn HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images, and fonts into pixels through parsing, style calculation, layout, paint, and compositing. Understanding that pipeline explains why some frontend changes are cheap while others cause jank.

nodes.label: Next.js App Router Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Next.js App Router organizes applications around nested route segments, layouts, React Server Components, server actions, loading states, and colocated metadata. Its core architectural decision is where code runs: server, client, build time, request time, or edge.

nodes.label: OpenGraph Protocol
nodes.excerpt: OpenGraph metadata controls how pages appear when shared on social platforms, chat apps, and link previews. Good titles, descriptions, images, URLs, and content types make links legible and trustworthy outside the site itself.

nodes.label: Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)
nodes.excerpt: Progressive Web Apps use web platform features to feel more app-like: installability, offline support, caching, background behavior, and responsive layouts. The core idea is progressive enhancement: the app should improve when capabilities exist without breaking when they do not.

nodes.label: React — Component Lifecycle
nodes.excerpt: React component lifecycle is the sequence of rendering, committing to the DOM, running effects, updating, and unmounting. In modern React, lifecycle thinking mostly appears through hooks, effects, refs, and cleanup behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Hooks Deep Dive (useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo)
nodes.label: React — Hooks Deep Dive (useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo)
nodes.excerpt: React hooks let function components hold state, run effects, preserve mutable refs, memoize calculations, and compose reusable behavior. The core skill is knowing which hook matches the problem rather than turning every concern into an effect.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Performance Optimization (memo, lazy, Suspense)
nodes.label: React — Performance Optimization (memo, lazy, Suspense)
nodes.excerpt: React performance optimization focuses on reducing unnecessary rendering, splitting code, managing expensive work, and improving perceived loading states. Tools like memo, lazy, and Suspense help when used for measured bottlenecks rather than as default decoration.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Reconciliation and Virtual DOM
nodes.label: React — Reconciliation and Virtual DOM
nodes.excerpt: React reconciliation compares a component tree's previous and next output to decide what host updates are needed. The virtual DOM is the intermediate description React uses to make UI updates predictable, batched, and component-driven.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — State Management (Zustand, Redux, Context API)
nodes.label: React — State Management (Zustand, Redux, Context API)
nodes.excerpt: React state management is about choosing where state lives, who can update it, and how changes propagate. Local state, Context, Zustand, Redux, URL state, and server cache tools solve different problems and should not be treated as one category.

nodes.label: Responsive Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Responsive design makes interfaces adapt to different screen sizes, input modes, zoom levels, content lengths, and device constraints. Good responsive design uses fluid layout, flexible media, stable constraints, and content-first breakpoints.

nodes.label: SEO Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: SEO fundamentals for web engineers center on crawlable content, clear metadata, semantic HTML, fast pages, canonical URLs, structured data, and useful content. Technical SEO makes it easier for search engines to discover, understand, and rank pages.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Server-Side Rendering vs Static Site Generation vs CSR
nodes.label: Server-Side Rendering vs Static Site Generation vs CSR
nodes.excerpt: SSR, SSG, and client-side rendering are different ways to produce HTML and interactive UI. The tradeoffs involve freshness, performance, caching, personalization, SEO, infrastructure cost, and client JavaScript weight.

nodes.label: Service Workers
nodes.excerpt: Service workers are background scripts that sit between a web app and the network. They enable offline support, request interception, caching strategies, background sync, and push notifications, but their lifecycle and cache invalidation rules require care.

nodes.label: Spring Physics in Animation
nodes.excerpt: Spring physics models animation as a system with tension, damping, mass, and velocity. It often feels more natural than fixed-duration easing because movement responds to distance and interruption.

nodes.label: Web Components
nodes.excerpt: Web Components are browser-native primitives for reusable custom elements, encapsulated markup, and scoped styling. They are useful for framework-agnostic components, design systems, and embeddable widgets, but require careful accessibility and integration design.

nodes.label: Web Performance Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Web performance optimization is about making pages load quickly, respond quickly, and stay visually stable under real user conditions. It combines network strategy, rendering strategy, JavaScript discipline, asset optimization, caching, and measurement.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Web Scraping Ethics and Techniques
nodes.label: Web Scraping Ethics and Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Web scraping combines HTTP, parsing, browser automation, rate limiting, and data cleaning, but it also carries legal, ethical, and operational responsibilities. Responsible scraping minimizes load, respects access controls, and avoids collecting sensitive data unnecessarily.

nodes.label: WebGL Basics
nodes.excerpt: WebGL is a browser API for rendering GPU-accelerated 2D and 3D graphics through an OpenGL-like pipeline. It powers interactive visualizations, games, effects, and 3D web experiences, usually through libraries such as Three.js.

nodes.label: What Is Obsidian
nodes.excerpt: what Obsidian is, what it can do, and the right workflow for this vault (discuss with AI first, then write clean notes).
